8	---
9	# Feature: Task Execution
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-010
12	**Status:** In Progress
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	DDx owns the task execution lifecycle. The lifecycle is made of concrete layers
19	with stable public names: `run`, `try`, and `work`. Each higher layer composes
20	the layer beneath it. There are no other run kinds beyond those named layers.
21	
22	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
23	|---|---|---|---|---|
24	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + execution config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream Fizeau execution contract |
25	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
26	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
27	
28	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk
29	substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
30	
31	Implementation code for this boundary should follow the task-execution
32	vocabulary (`taskexec` for core layer logic, `fizeauadapter` for the service
33	bridge) rather than the legacy `internal/agent` package name. Those package
34	names should make the DDx-owned workflow boundary explicit.
35	
36	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 when the
37	generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata. It is
38	not a fourth layer — it is a labeled invocation at an existing layer.
39	
40	## Problem Statement
41	
42	**Current situation:** DDx has accreted two independent run-storage shapes
43	— `.ddx/exec-runs/` for generic execution evidence and
44	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` for tracked bead-attempt bundles. Loop behavior,
45	worktree lifecycle, and bead resolution are spread across legacy command
46	surfaces without a clean layering story. There is no single place to introspect "what runs
47	happened, at what layer, with what evidence."
48	
49	**Pain points:**
50	- Two on-disk record shapes for fundamentally similar artifacts (a single
51	  agent invocation, with logs, structured result, and provenance)
52	- No first-class concept of the task execution layers (`run` / `try` /
53	  `work`), so consumers must intuit which surface to read
54	- Stop conditions for queue drain are implicit and not observable
55	- Generated-artifact provenance has no clean hook into the run record
56	- Open-ended catalog pressure: every new "run flavor" (benchmark, replay,
57	  comparison) tempts a new on-disk shape or a new top-level verb
58	
59	**Desired outcome:** A single on-disk run substrate with explicit layer
60	metadata, three top-level CLI verbs that map 1:1 onto the layers, and a
61	narrow read-only HTTP/MCP write surface limited to artifact regeneration.
62	
63	## Task Execution Layers
64	
65	### Layer 1 — `ddx run` (invocation atom)
66	
67	A layer-1 run is one AI invocation. Inputs are a prompt, requested
68	`MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`, optional operator passthrough constraints,
69	and non-routing execution config; outputs are the structured response (text or
70	bytes), any side-effects the service performed via tools, and run metadata
71	(tokens, model, actual power, duration, exit status, session pointer).
72	
73	Layer 1 calls the upstream Fizeau execution contract directly. DDx does not
74	reimplement routing or the invocation loop; it wraps one `Execute` call with
75	provenance capture.
76	
77	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is layer 1 when the generator returns
78	bytes/text and DDx writes the file; it is layer 2 when the generator
79	edits the repo directly (bead-less worktree).
80	
81	### Layer 2 — `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt)
82	
83	A layer-2 run is one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. It owns:
84	
85	- Worktree creation from a base revision and worktree finalization
86	  (merge or preserve)
87	- Worktree cleanup after finalization, failed setup, failed publish, and
88	  ordinary interruption
89	- Bead → prompt resolution (description, acceptance, governing artifacts)
90	- Side-effect bundling (commits, evidence, no-changes rationale)
91	- One or more layer-1 invocations recorded inside the attempt record
92	
93	A layer-2 record references its child layer-1 records by run id.
94	
95	Layer 2 owns bead-attempt success classification. DDx determines success from
96	the artifacts it owns: commit presence, merge/preserve result, no-changes
97	rationale, post-run checks, review verdicts, and cooldown policy. The agent's
98	exit status and actual model/power are inputs to that decision, not the whole
99	decision.
100	
101	Layer 2 records raw attempt evidence. Layer 3 applies
102	[`TD-031`](../../02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md) when
103	that evidence requires durable bead action such as close-as-already-satisfied,
104	human triage, blocked, retry cooldown, or stale no_changes metadata cleanup.
105	
106	### Quality hooks
107	
108	ADR-023 defines two lifecycle quality hooks owned by the layer-2 and layer-3
109	execution loop. They are hooks inside `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, not new run
110	layers and not new bead-schema fields.
111	
112	`BeadReadinessHook` is the product concept: it runs after the worker has
113	selected a dependency-ready candidate but before DDx claims the bead or
114	creates the implementation worktree. The canonical decision being made is bead
115	readiness assessment, the pre-claim check for tractability and actionability;
116	lint/rubric scoring is the diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt
117	triage is a separate after-evidence action.
118	The implementation may still call the compatibility entrypoint
119	`PreClaimIntakeHook` and record `MODE: intake` for legacy compatibility, but
120	those names are aliases only. Within readiness, the nested bead-lifecycle
121	workflow skill performs lint/rubric scoring to support the decision, and the
122	score is diagnostic evidence rather than a separate queue action. Post-attempt
123	triage is separate and runs only after evidence exists. The hook has enough
124	context to evaluate title, description, acceptance criteria, labels, parent,
125	deps, bead type, spec-id, prior attempt history, and whether the bead is
126	atomic enough to execute. The hook invokes the nested bead-lifecycle workflow
127	skill under the `ddx` skill tree and records readiness evidence in the
128	layer-3 run record.
129	
130	`ddx work` wires this hook by default in both CLI and server-managed worker
131	paths. Decomposition decisions run with a strong `MinPower` floor, defaulting to
132	the smart/top-power tier floor when no project-specific splitter override is
133	configured. DDx does not choose the concrete model; it passes the raised power
134	floor to Fizeau and preserves any operator-supplied harness/provider/model
135	passthrough constraints. If those constraints cannot satisfy the strong floor,
136	DDx records `agent_power_unsatisfied` and blocks instead of running weak
137	decomposition.
138	
139	The readiness assessment result is one of:
140	
141	- `actionable_atomic` — claim and execute normally.
142	- `actionable_but_rewritten` — DDx applied safe, intent-preserving bead updates
143	  such as formatting the description into the authoring template, adding
144	  discovered file:line evidence, adding an obvious test command, or wiring
145	  deterministic labels/parent/deps. The update is recorded through `ddx bead`
146	  mutation paths before claim.
147	- `too_large_decomposed` — DDx created child beads, mapped every parent AC to
148	  child ACs or an explicit `needs_human` / `non_scope` marker, blocked the
149	  parent, and did not execute the parent.
150	- `ambiguous_needs_human` — the bead/spec is unclear, contradictory,
151	  unverifiable, or missing acceptance criteria that DDx cannot safely invent.
152	  DDx blocks or marks the bead `needs_human` and does not claim it.
153	- `readiness_error` — readiness infrastructure failed. In migration/WARN mode
154	  this fails open with evidence; in reliable factory/BLOCK mode it may skip the
155	  candidate for the current pass but must not park it behind a cooldown unless a
156	  retryable time-based class is recorded.
157	
158	Safe rewrites may clarify existing intent; they must not invent product
159	behavior, choose between conflicting requirements, change scope, or guess a
160	missing governing artifact. Those cases become `ambiguous_needs_human`.
161	
162	`PostAttemptTriageHook` runs after the attempt has produced its local evidence:
163	agent result, commit/no-commit state, no-changes rationale if any, post-run
164	checks, adversarial review verdict, merge/preserve result, and the readiness
165	report.
166	It uses the same bead-lifecycle skill to classify whether the outcome is a
167	normal attempt result, a quality-policy failure, a missing-evidence failure, or
168	an infrastructure failure. This is distinct from bead readiness assessment and
169	from the lint/rubric scoring used inside readiness. The hook must never rewrite
170	the attempt result or erase artifacts; it only adds triage evidence and feeds
171	retry/stop classification.
172	
173	If post-attempt triage finds that an implementation attempt stopped because the
174	bead was too large or the worker could not legally decompose inside its
175	worktree/depth context, the layer-3 worker must invoke the same orchestrator
176	decomposition path used by `BeadReadinessHook`. This is machine-actionable
177	work: DDx files child beads, records the AC map, and blocks the parent unless
178	the split is lossy, ambiguous, or at the queue-level decomposition depth cap.
179	The operator is not required merely because the implementer could not split
180	from inside the attempted execution.
181	
182	`ExecuteBeadReport` gains `OutcomeReason` beside the existing `Disrupted`
183	signal. `Disrupted` remains the mechanical indicator that normal completion was
184	interrupted. `OutcomeReason` is the stable classification string that explains
185	why the attempt ended, such as `lint_warn`, `lint_blocked`,
186	`lint_hook_error_fail_open`, `readiness_ambiguous`, `readiness_decomposed`,
187	`triage_missing_evidence`, `review_fixable_gap`, `review_spec_gap`,
188	`review_too_large`, `post_run_check_failed`, or `success`. Layer-3 `ddx work`
189	records aggregate these reasons when evaluating retry eligibility, no-progress,
190	blocked, and deferred stop conditions.
191	
192	The hook evidence lives in the attempt evidence bundle and is surfaced through
193	the unified run substrate. The originating bead may receive an evidence event
194	only for explicit operator override (`--force --reason`) or other existing
195	audit events; lint scores are not copied into `.ddx/beads.jsonl`.
196	
197	### Layer 3 — `ddx work` (queue drain)
198	
199	A layer-3 run is one drain of the bead queue. It iterates `ddx try`
200	across ready beads until a stop condition is met. It owns:
201	
202	- Queue iteration order
203	- Pre-claim readiness, safe bead improvement, decomposition, claim acquisition,
204	  claim release, and shutdown/interruption cleanup for claimed beads, using the
205	  TD-031 claim-state contract
206	- Durable bead action after each layer-2 attempt, using TD-031's outcome,
207	  no_changes, cooldown, and stale-metadata rules
208	- No-progress / stop-condition evaluation
209	- A loop-level record that references its child layer-2 records by
210	  attempt id and reports terminal disposition (drained, blocked,
211	  deferred, no-progress)
212	
213	Content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue
214	reconciliation beads") are not layer-3 concerns. Those are skill or
215	plugin compositions on top of the task execution layers.
216	
217	Layer 3 treats raw attempt evidence separately from durable bead action. When a
218	worker stops, receives SIGTERM/SIGINT, loses its child agent process, or honors
219	operator cancel before a terminal bead mutation, it follows TD-031: preserve
220	evidence, record the interruption/disruption, release the claim, and leave the
221	bead re-claimable unless an explicit retryable cooldown or blocker was recorded.
222	Shutdown is not itself a reason to park a bead on `execute-loop-retry-after`.
223	
224	## Agent Power and Retry
225	
226	DDx owns retry policy between bead attempts. If an attempt produces classified
227	evidence that more capable reasoning could plausibly change the result, DDx may
228	retry the bead with a higher `MinPower`.
229	
230	Fizeau owns routing within the requested power bounds. A retry request says
231	"use at least this much power" by raising `MinPower`; an explicit `MaxPower`, if
232	supplied, remains a hard upper bound. Operator-supplied `--harness`,
233	`--provider`, and `--model` values may be sent to Fizeau as passthrough
234	constraints, but DDx does not interpret them. Each layer-1 record stores:
235	
236	- requested `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`
237	- requested passthrough constraints, if any
238	- actual model and actual power reported by Fizeau
239	- run outcome and DDx attempt outcome
240	
241	DDx may query Fizeau's available model/power catalog to choose escalation
242	targets such as "retry using only top-power models." DDx uses the returned power
243	numbers only to compute `MinPower` thresholds; Fizeau still chooses the
244	concrete model.
245	
246	Passthrough constraints stay sticky across retries. DDx may increase `MinPower`
247	between attempts, but it must not alter or drop operator-supplied
248	harness/provider/model passthrough values to make escalation work. If the
249	combination is invalid or constraining, the agent owns that typed error or
250	actual route. DDx stops with `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or
251	`agent_power_unsatisfied`, records the evidence, and reports operator action
252	required; it does not remove pins, widen pins, call `ResolveRoute`, or retry in a
253	loop.
254	
255	### Retry eligibility
256	
257	Power retry is eligible only when DDx-owned evidence shows that a stronger model
258	could plausibly help after a valid attempt started. Eligible classes include:
259	
260	- `capability_insufficient` — the agent attempted the bead, but reasoning
261	  quality or implementation quality was insufficient.
262	- `post_run_check_failed` — tests or gates failed after a valid checkout and
263	  attempted change.
264	- `review_fixable_gap` — adversarial review found implementation or test gaps
265	  plausibly addressable by another automated cycle.
266	- `no_changes_after_attempt` — the agent had a valid checkout and task context
267	  but produced no usable change, subject to the no-progress budget.
268	
269	Power retry is not eligible for deterministic setup or operator-action
270	failures:
271	
272	- dirty worktree, merge/land conflict, missing checkout, invalid bead metadata,
273	  unresolved dependencies, config parse errors, missing harness binaries,
274	  authentication failures, command-not-found/toolchain setup failures
275	- readiness ambiguity, missing acceptance criteria, spec contradictions,
276	  decomposition overflow, review errors, reviewer context overflow, claim races,
277	  routing preflight rejection, quota/transport disruption, and any other class
278	  where the implementer did not receive valid task context and an opportunity to
279	  act
280	- `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` / `agent_power_unsatisfied`
281	- any status where retry would require DDx to inspect, remove, rewrite, or
282	  substitute `--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`
283	
284	Every retry/stop decision records the classification and evidence used. The
285	policy may inspect DDx attempt outcomes and the agent's typed status; it must
286	not branch on concrete provider/model identity.
287	
288	ADR-024 is the decision record for this policy. FEAT-010 owns when `ddx try` or
289	`ddx work` schedules a retry, how no-progress and stop conditions are recorded,
290	how review-error retry scopes are counted, and how budget caps stop a drain.
291	FEAT-006 owns only the agent request envelope used by those decisions.
292	
293	### Execution Cycle Traceability
294	
295	A bead execution is an append-only chain of implementation/review cycles. Layer
296	3 owns the chain; layer 2 owns the evidence for each cycle. A repair attempt
297	must create a new cycle record rather than overwrite the rejected one.
298	
299	Each cycle records:
300	
301	- `cycle_index`
302	- `attempt_id`
303	- `base_rev`
304	- `result_rev` when a stable candidate exists
305	- implementer layer-1 run ids, requested power bounds, actual route/power, and
306	  cost/usage facts
307	- verification command output and exit status
308	- `review_group_id`, reviewer layer-1 run ids, reviewer requested/actual
309	  route facts, aggregate verdict, per-AC evidence summary, and reviewer cost
310	- prior review linkage (`repair_context_from_review_group`) when this cycle is
311	  a repair attempt
312	- final cycle decision: close, retry, decompose, block, manual-required, or
313	  budget stop
314	
315	When a review requires another implementation cycle, the next implementation
316	prompt includes the prior review group's findings as required repair context.
317	The prior cycle remains immutable so operators can answer what changed, why the
318	first result was rejected, and why the final result passed.
319	
320	### Escalation, fallback, retry, and review decision tree
321	
322	The layer-3 drain evaluates each ready bead through this mechanical sequence:
323	
324	1. **Eligibility and readiness.** Pick a dependency-ready candidate. Run the
325	   readiness gate. Safe rewrites happen before claim. Too-large work
326	   is decomposed before an implementation attempt. Ambiguous or underspecified
327	   work is blocked with `needs_human`. Readiness infrastructure failure records
328	   evidence and follows the configured fail-open/factory-mode policy; it never
329	   creates an unexplained cooldown.
330	2. **Claim.** Claim only an `actionable_atomic` or safely rewritten bead. Claim
331	   races skip the bead for the current pass without cooldown.
332	3. **Primary implementation cycle.** Run one layer-2 implementation attempt.
333	   A candidate result records `base_rev`, `result_rev`, implementation run ids,
334	   route/power facts, verification output, and cost. Already-satisfied
335	   no-changes may close only after the required verification evidence exists;
336	   otherwise no-changes outcomes follow TD-031.
337	4. **Adversarial pre-close review.** Review is enabled by default. For every
338	   close-eligible `result_rev`, layer 2 dispatches two no-tool reviewer runs
339	   with `role=reviewer`, a stronger `MinPower` floor than the implementer, and
340	   correlation metadata (`review_group_id`, `result_rev`, reviewer slot, and
341	   implementer route facts). Close is permitted only when the aggregate review
342	   is unanimous `APPROVE` with per-AC evidence.
343	5. **Review classification.** Any evidenced `REQUEST_CHANGES` or `BLOCK`
344	   prevents close. `review_fixable_gap` schedules a repair cycle on the same
345	   bead when retry budgets allow, injecting the review findings as required
346	   repair context and optionally raising `MinPower`; it preserves the retry
347	   path and does not park the bead in the `needs_human` lane. `review_spec_gap`,
348	   `review_missing_acceptance`, `review_too_large`, and non-mechanical unsafe or
349	   out-of-scope findings park the bead in the `needs_human` operator-attention
350	   lane, clear the active claim, and do not ask another implementer to guess.
351	   Malformed, empty, context-overflow, and transport reviewer failures emit
352	   `review-error` scoped to `result_rev` and reviewer slot; after
353	   `review_max_retries` they emit `review-manual-required`, clear the active
354	   claim, park the bead in the `needs_human` lane, and do not close. The
355	   `review_fixable_gap` path stays on the automatic retry track instead of
356	   converting into operator review.
357	6. **Infrastructure fallback.** Transport, quota, rate-limit, command setup,
358	   context cancellation, routing preflight rejection, and worker disruption are
359	   not model-capability failures. They emit structured evidence and either stay
360	   immediately retryable or use a bounded retry-after when the same class is
361	   time-based. HTTP 429 retry happens inside one attempt with `rate-limit-retry`
362	   events.
363	7. **Capability retry.** Failed checks, `review_fixable_gap`,
364	   capability-insufficient attempts, and eligible no-changes-after-attempt may
365	   schedule a higher-power retry by raising the next request's `MinPower`.
366	   Passthrough constraints remain unchanged. If the requested power cannot be
367	   satisfied under those constraints, DDx records a terminal operator-action
368	   classification and stops retrying that bead.
369	8. **Cost stop.** Implementation and review attempts both contribute reported
370	   billable cost to the drain budget according to FEAT-014 cost-class metadata.
371	   When the cap trips, the drain records a budget stop and stops claiming new
372	   work; the cap is not reported as model failure.
373	
374	## Substrate Unification
375	
376	### One record shape
377	
378	All task execution layers persist to a single on-disk shape under
379	`.ddx/runs/<run-id>/`. The record has the following common fields and a
380	discriminating `layer` field; layer-specific extensions live in named
381	subobjects rather than separate file trees.
382	
383	Common fields:
384	- `run_id` — stable, sortable identifier
385	- `layer` — `1` | `2` | `3`
386	- `parent_run_id` — pointer to the enclosing layer's run, if any
387	- `started_at` / `finished_at`
388	- `terminal_status` — `success` | `failure` | `timeout` | `error` |
389	  `no_progress` (layer 3 only) | `preserved` (layer 2 only)
390	- `actor`, `host`, `git_revision`, `ddx_version`
391	- `produces_artifact` — optional artifact id when this run regenerates
392	  an artifact
393	- `attachments[]` — references to large bodies (logs, structured payloads,
394	  bundles) stored alongside the record rather than inline
395	
396	Layer extensions:
397	- `layer1` — prompt reference, agent config, model id, token usage,
398	  upstream session id, structured response pointer
399	- `layer2` — bead id, base revision, worktree path, finalization mode
400	  (`merge` | `preserve`), child layer-1 run ids, evidence-bundle pointer
401	- `layer3` — queue snapshot pointer, stop-condition evaluation log,
402	  child layer-2 attempt ids, drain disposition
403	
404	Records are append-only. A new run never rewrites a previous one.
405	
406	### On-disk migration
407	
408	The two pre-existing trees collapse into the unified substrate:
409	
410	- `.ddx/exec-runs/<run-id>/` → `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 1`
411	- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` → `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 2`
412	  and the existing prompt / manifest / result / checks / provenance
413	  surfaced as layer-2 evidence-bundle attachments
414	
415	Layer-3 records are new (no pre-existing tree).
416	
417	Migration is one-way and read-compatible:
418	- Writers always target `.ddx/runs/`
419	- Readers tolerate legacy paths during a documented migration window
420	  and surface them through the unified read API as if they were layer-1
421	  or layer-2 records, then a one-shot rewrite normalizes them on disk
422	- After the migration window closes, legacy paths are removed; readers
423	  no longer fall back
424	
425	The migration window, the rewrite tool, and the cutoff signal are
426	specified in the implementation epic, not here.
427	
428	## `ddx work` Stop Conditions
429	
430	`ddx work` drains the queue until **any** of the following stop
431	conditions is met. The terminal disposition of the layer-3 record names
432	which condition fired.
433	
434	1. **`drained`** — queue is empty (no ready beads remain).
435	2. **`blocked`** — every remaining ready bead has produced a terminal
436	   non-success outcome on its most recent attempt and re-attempt is not
437	   permitted under the current policy.
438	3. **`deferred`** — a configured wall-clock or attempt-count budget is
439	   exhausted. The remaining queue is intact and a subsequent `ddx work`
440	   resumes from where this one stopped.
441	4. **`no_progress`** — `N` consecutive valid implementation attempts produced
442	   no commit and no merged side-effect on the base branch (default `N = 3`;
443	   configurable). A valid implementation attempt is one where DDx created a
444	   checkout, supplied task context to the implementer, and the implementer had
445	   an opportunity to act. Intake blocks, decomposition, claim races, routing
446	   preflight rejection, quota/transport/auth/tool setup failures, review errors,
447	   and operator-action classes do not increment this counter.
448	5. **`signal`** — a SIGINT/SIGTERM was received. On the first signal, DDx
449	   prints `Cancel received, shutting down gracefully`, cancels the active
450	   command context, stops claiming new beads, and gives the in-flight
451	   `ddx try` (if any) one cooperative cleanup path to preserve evidence,
452	   release its claim, and leave the bead re-claimable. A second signal may
453	   hard-abort the process.
454	6. **`resource_exhausted`** — DDx cannot safely create or publish execution
455	   state because a required execution root is out of bytes, out of inodes, not
456	   writable, or cannot register/remove git worktrees. Resource exhaustion is
457	   host infrastructure failure, not bead failure and not model no-progress.
458	   `ddx work` stops claiming new beads after recording the failed `ddx try`
459	   setup evidence and a cleanup summary. It must not continue to the next
460	   ready bead in the same drain.
461	
462	Stop-condition evaluation normally runs **between** `ddx try` invocations.
463	`signal` is the exception: the first signal cancels an in-flight attempt
464	cooperatively rather than waiting for the model to finish. The cancelled
465	attempt is mechanically disrupted, not a model no-progress failure, and ordinary
466	interrupt shutdown MUST NOT park the bead behind `execute-loop-retry-after`.
467	
468	### Execution Resource Cleanup
469	
470	DDx-created execution resources must have an owner, a liveness signal, a
471	retention policy, and a cleanup path. Missing ownership, missing liveness, or
472	unbounded accumulation is a correctness bug in the execution substrate.
473	
474	Layer 2 owns inline cleanup for one `ddx try` attempt. Before claim or worktree
475	creation it validates the required execution roots:
476	
477	- the temporary worktree root, currently `$TMPDIR/ddx-exec-wt` unless
478	  overridden
479	- the durable evidence root, currently `.ddx/executions` during migration and
480	  `.ddx/runs` for new substrate records
481	- git worktree registration/removal for the project repository
482	
483	The validation checks writability, free bytes, and free inodes where the
484	platform exposes inode counts. If validation fails, `ddx try` runs one
485	immediate DDx-scoped cleanup pass, re-checks, and then either proceeds or
486	returns `resource_exhausted` without claiming the bead.
487	
488	`ddx work` and server-managed workers use the same cleanup manager before the
489	first claim and before later claims whenever temp free space drops below a soft
490	high-water threshold. If cleanup does not restore the temp roots above the hard
491	floor, the loop stops visibly with `resource_exhausted` and claims no more
492	beads.
493	
494	After an attempt starts, Layer 2 removes the isolated worktree when the result
495	has been merged, explicitly preserved, classified as no-changes/no-evidence, or
496	interrupted through the cooperative shutdown path. Failed setup must remove any
497	partial unregistered directory it created. A worktree may remain only when DDx
498	records an explicit preserve decision with evidence pointing at the retained
499	path or ref.
500	
501	DDx-owned cleanup scope includes execution worktrees, DDx-created test and e2e
502	scratch roots, generated test binaries, and run-state or liveness files. The
503	cleanup manager may delete only DDx-owned paths with ownership metadata or
504	known DDx prefixes, minimum age/mtime satisfied, and no live PID/session
505	liveness. It must preserve published evidence, registered active worktrees,
506	and anything outside DDx-owned roots.
507	
508	Layer 3 owns loop cleanup. `ddx work` runs cleanup:
509	
510	- once at startup, before the first queue claim
511	- before the next claim after any setup/finalization failure
512	- periodically while a long-lived poll worker remains active
513	- during graceful signal shutdown before exit
514	
515	Long-lived DDx processes also start a background cleanup worker. The background
516	worker runs occasionally with jitter and a project-level cleanup lock so multiple
517	workers do not all prune at once. It is conservative and DDx-scoped: it may
518	remove stale unregistered directories under DDx temp roots, registered DDx
519	worktrees whose attempt is terminal or whose liveness marker is stale, stale
520	heartbeat/liveness files for dead PIDs, and partial setup directories that were
521	never published as complete evidence. It must not remove preserved worktrees,
522	`refs/ddx/iterations/...`, complete `.ddx/runs/<id>` or
523	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>` evidence, active worktrees with live
524	PID/session liveness, or non-DDx directories.
525	
526	Cleanup is observable but not noisy. Routine passes are trace/debug or worker
527	events. Passes that reclaim significant bytes or inodes emit an operator-visible
528	summary such as `cleanup: removed 37 stale ddx worktrees, freed 14210 inodes`.
529	Resource exhaustion after cleanup is a hard visible stop message and a layer-3
530	`resource_exhausted` disposition.
531	
532	Cleanup reporting includes scratch roots removed, bytes and inodes reclaimed,
533	preserved paths, and blocked warnings so operators can see why cleanup stopped
534	short.
535	
536	### Long-running default (`--poll-interval`)
537	
538	Per ddx-dc157075, the default `--poll-interval` for `ddx work` is **30s**.
539	With a positive poll interval the
540	worker stays alive across empty polls (`drained` becomes a transient
541	"running (idle)" substate, not a terminal exit). The loop exits only on
542	the conditions enumerated above plus `signal`, fatal config errors, or
543	the explicit operator opt-outs:
544	
545	- `--once` — process at most one ready bead, then exit.
546	- `--poll-interval=0` — legacy "drain-and-exit" semantics: when the
547	  queue empties, return immediately without polling.
548	
549	Server-managed workers spawned via `POST /api/workers/execute-loop`
550	inherit the same 30s default when the request omits `poll_interval`. The
551	worker record exposes a `substate` field set to `"idle"` while the loop
552	is sleeping between empty polls; it is cleared as soon as a candidate is
553	picked. Terminal worker states (`exited`, `failed`, `stopped`, `reaped`)
554	always clear `substate`.
555	
556	The structured `loop.end` event carries an `exit_reason` field
557	(`once_complete`, `explicit_poll_zero`, `sigint`, `sigterm`,
558	`fatal_config`, `resource_exhausted`; `providers_exhausted` is reserved for the
559	quota-pause work in ddx-aede917d).
560	
561	Power escalation is evaluated between attempts as part of retry policy. A
562	higher-power retry raises `MinPower` and resets neither the evidence history nor
563	the no-progress counter; it is recorded as the next `ddx try` with its own
564	requested bounds and actual power metadata.
565	
566	The evaluation log is persisted on the layer-3 record so a human or
567	tool can audit which condition fired and on which iteration.
568	
569	## Quality Hooks
570	
571	ADR-023 (`../../02-design/adr/ADR-023-bead-lifecycle-quality-policy.md`)
572	adds two quality hooks to the layer-2/layer-3 lifecycle. The hooks are
573	implemented in `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime` at the same boundary that already
574	owns bead selection, attempt finalization, and retry classification.
575	
576	`BeadReadinessHook` runs after a bead has been selected and verified as
577	dependency-eligible, but before DDx claims it or creates the implementation
578	worktree. It performs the canonical bead readiness assessment, the pre-claim
579	decision about tractability and actionability; lint/rubric scoring is the
580	diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt triage is a separate
581	after-evidence action. The legacy compatibility name `PreClaimIntakeHook` and
582	`MODE: intake` may still appear in migration code or notes, but they are not
583	the product concept.
584	
585	The hook receives the bead record, current execution policy, hook mode
586	(`WARN-ONLY` or `BLOCK`), and the layer-3 evidence directory. It invokes the
587	bead-lifecycle workflow skill from FEAT-011 and writes a bead readiness,
588	scope, and decomposition report. In WARN-ONLY mode, the report is diagnostic
589	unless the hook can safely improve or decompose the bead. In BLOCK/factory
590	mode, a valid low readiness score, unsafe ambiguity, or too-large
591	classification stops implementation before claim.
592	
593	`PostAttemptTriageHook` runs after the attempt has produced its owned
594	evidence: commits or no-changes rationale, command results, review verdicts,
595	merge/preserve result, and any structural validation result. It invokes the
596	same bead-lifecycle workflow skill against the attempt bundle and returns an
597	attempt classification such as prompt-quality issue, task-quality issue,
598	infrastructure failure, deterministic setup failure, or operator action
599	required. This classification feeds retry reporting and operator UX; it does
600	not replace the existing success/no-changes/failed outcome taxonomy.
601	
602	Both hooks are fail-open for infrastructure failures. If the skill package is
603	missing, the hook process crashes, evidence cannot be written, or the model
604	invocation fails, DDx records the hook error and continues with the underlying
605	attempt flow in WARN-ONLY mode. In BLOCK/factory mode, hook infrastructure
606	failure may skip the current candidate for the pass with observable evidence,
607	but it must not park the bead behind an unexplained cooldown. Only a
608	successfully computed readiness/lint result can block dispatch as an authoring
609	or scope failure. `--force --reason <text>` records an event with the actor,
610	reason, hook mode, and overridden criteria before proceeding.
611	
612	Long-running entrypoints perform a lightweight DDx runtime preflight before
613	starting work. This is not a full `ddx doctor` run: it avoids network checks,
614	package-manager checks, route probing, stale-document scans, and other broad
615	diagnostics. The preflight is limited to conditions that can predictably degrade
616	the command about to run:
617	
618	- project root resolution;
619	- `.ddx/config.yaml` parseability;
620	- `.ddx/beads.jsonl` parseability for bead-execution commands;
621	- the root `ddx` project skill under `.agents/skills/ddx/SKILL.md` or
622	  `.claude/skills/ddx/SKILL.md`;
623	- the nested `bead-lifecycle` skill when readiness/lint/triage hooks are wired;
624	- legacy project-local skill symlinks from the pre-FEAT-015 install model.
625	
626	Passing preflight checks are silent. Failed checks are observable and
627	actionable. `ddx work`, `ddx try`, and compatibility `ddx agent execute-loop`
628	fail on missing required project/tracker state, but warn once per process and
629	continue for optional lifecycle-hook degradation in WARN-ONLY mode. `ddx server`
630	fails on missing required server project state, but missing lifecycle skills or
631	legacy skill layout are surfaced as degraded startup diagnostics and health
632	metadata rather than blocking the HTTP server. When the lifecycle skill is
633	missing, both startup preflight and hook-time errors must include the project
634	root, the exact checked paths, and remediation:
635	
636	```text
637	Run:
638	  ddx update --force
639	  ddx doctor
640	```
641	
642	`ExecuteBeadReport` gains an `OutcomeReason` field beside the existing
643	`Disrupted` flag. `Disrupted` remains the coarse boolean that the attempt did
644	not complete normally. `OutcomeReason` is the machine-readable reason selected
645	by lifecycle classification, for example:
646	
647	- `bead_lint_warn`
648	- `bead_lint_blocked`
649	- `readiness_actionable_atomic`
650	- `readiness_rewritten`
651	- `readiness_decomposed`
652	- `readiness_needs_human`
653	- `forced_with_reason`
654	- `triage_prompt_quality`
655	- `triage_task_quality`
656	- `triage_infrastructure_failure`
657	- `triage_operator_action`
658	- `hook_unavailable_fail_open`
659	
660	Layer-3 `ddx work` records include the hook summaries for each child layer-2
661	attempt in the stop-condition evaluation log. A blocked lint result counts as a
662	terminal non-success outcome for that bead under the current policy, so the
663	existing `blocked`, `deferred`, and `no_progress` loop rules can reason about
664	it without a fourth run layer or a separate run type.
665	
666	## No Run-Type Catalog
667	
668	DDx will not introduce additional run kinds beyond `run`, `try`, and `work`.
669	Comparison, replay, benchmark, adversarial review, effort estimate,
670	bead breakdown, and similar workflows are **skill compositions** over
671	the task execution layers — they emit ordinary layer-1, layer-2, or layer-3
672	records, optionally tagged with a free-form `skill` label, and do not get
673	bespoke storage shapes, bespoke verbs, or bespoke spec sections.
674	
675	This is a load-bearing non-goal: every future "we should add a fourth
676	layer for X" proposal is rejected by reference to this section. If a
677	new workflow cannot be expressed as a composition over `run` / `try` /
678	`work`, the gap is in the task execution contract, not in the catalog.
679	
680	## Requirements
681	
682	### Functional
683	
684	1. **One on-disk substrate** — all run records, regardless of layer,
685	   persist under `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with the common record shape and
686	   a discriminating `layer` field.
687	2. **Layer metadata** — every run record carries `layer`,
688	   `parent_run_id`, and the layer-appropriate extension subobject.
689	3. **Layer-1 invocation** — `ddx run` produces exactly one layer-1
690	   record per invocation; consumes upstream Fizeau execution contract
691	   per CONTRACT-003.
692	4. **Layer-2 attempt** — `ddx try <bead>` produces exactly one layer-2
693	   record, references its child layer-1 records, and finalizes the
694	   worktree as `merge` or `preserve`.
695	5. **Layer-3 drain** — `ddx work` produces exactly one layer-3 record
696	   per invocation, references its child layer-2 attempt ids, and reports
697	   one of the terminal dispositions (`drained`, `blocked`, `deferred`,
698	   `no_progress`, `signal`, `resource_exhausted`).
699	6. **Stop conditions** — `ddx work` evaluates the stop conditions
700	   between attempts and persists the evaluation log on the layer-3
701	   record.
702	7. **No-progress detection** — the `no_progress` condition is mechanical
703	   (no commit, no merged side-effect) and configurable via
704	   `.ddx/config.yaml` (default `N = 3`).
705	8. **Append-only history** — a new run never rewrites a previous record
706	   at any layer.
707	9. **Cross-layer query** — `ddx runs` lists records across all three
708	   layers with layer filtering; `ddx tries` and `ddx work workers`
709	   are layer-specific projections over the same substrate.
710	10. **Run detail inspection** — for any run id, callers can retrieve the
711	    common fields, the layer extension, attachments, and child run ids.
712	11. **Generated-artifact provenance** — runs that regenerate an artifact
713	    set `produces_artifact: <id>` on the run record; FEAT-007 reads this
714	    to populate `generated_by` graph edges.
715	12. **Migration from legacy paths** — readers tolerate
716	    `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` during a
717	    documented migration window and surface them as layer-1 / layer-2
718	    records; writers always target `.ddx/runs/`.
719	13. **Configuration** — substrate root, no-progress threshold, deferred
720	    budget, and retention settings are configurable in
721	    `.ddx/config.yaml`.
722	14. **Execution cleanup** — `ddx try` and `ddx work` remove stale DDx-owned
723	    execution worktrees, DDx-created test/e2e scratch roots, generated test
724	    binaries, and liveness files through inline, loop, and background cleanup
725	    paths without deleting preserved attempts or published evidence.
726	15. **Resource preflight** — `ddx try` validates writable execution roots and
727	    free bytes/inodes before claim; failed validation may trigger one cleanup
728	    retry, and `ddx work` / server-managed workers run cleanup before claim
729	    whenever temp free space drops below a soft high-water threshold. If the
730	    temp roots remain below the hard floor after cleanup, the loop returns
731	    `resource_exhausted` without claiming.
732	
733	Expected implementation tests include `TestExecutionCleanup_RemovesStaleDDXScratchDirs`,
734	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_RunsCleanupBelowSoftFloor`, and
735	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_StopsBelowHardFloorAfterCleanup`.
736	
737	### Non-Functional
738	
739	- **Determinism:** the persisted record reflects the exact prompt,
740	  response, logs, and structured result of one invocation.
741	- **Durability:** record writes are atomic or serialized so concurrent
742	  writers cannot leave partial records.
743	- **Observability:** stored records are human-readable enough for
744	  debugging and machine-parseable enough for automation.
745	- **Portability:** records remain repo-local and file-backed; no hosted
746	  service or database is required.
747	- **Low overhead:** layer-1 record bookkeeping adds minimal overhead
748	  beyond the underlying agent invocation.
749	- **Payload resilience:** large prompt, response, log, and bundle
750	  bodies are stored as attachments and never require rewriting a shared
751	  history file to persist one run safely.
752	
753	## CLI Commands
754	
755	The three top-level verbs map 1:1 onto the layers:
756	
757	```bash
758	ddx run --prompt <file> [--harness <name>] [--provider <name>] [--model <name>]  # layer 1
759	ddx try <bead-id> [--from <rev>] [--no-merge]           # layer 2
760	ddx work [--workers N] [--budget <duration>]            # layer 3
761	```
762	
763	Cross-layer evidence introspection lives under `ddx runs`:
764	
765	```bash
766	ddx runs list [--layer 1|2|3] [--artifact ID] [--bead ID]
767	ddx runs show <run-id>                                  # full record + layer extension
768	ddx runs log <run-id>                                   # raw logs / attachments
769	ddx runs result <run-id> [--json]                       # structured result
770	```
771	
772	Layer-specific projections:
773	
774	```bash
775	ddx tries list [--bead ID]                              # layer-2 only
776	ddx tries show <attempt-id>
777	ddx work workers list                                   # layer-3 worker mgmt
778	```
779	
780	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over `ddx run` (or `ddx try`
781	when the generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>`

784	## Server Endpoints (FEAT-002 integration)
785	
786	| MCP Tool | HTTP Endpoint | Description | Mode |
787	|----------|--------------|-------------|------|
788	| `ddx_runs_list` | `GET /api/runs` | List run records across layers, filterable by `layer`, `artifact`, `bead` | read |
789	| `ddx_runs_show` | `GET /api/runs/:id` | Show one run record with layer extension and attachment refs | read |
790	| `ddx_runs_log` | `GET /api/runs/:id/log` | Raw logs / attachment bodies | read |
791	| `ddx_runs_result` | `GET /api/runs/:id/result` | Structured result payload | read |
792	| `ddx_artifact_regenerate` | `POST /api/artifacts/:id/regenerate` | Trigger regeneration of one artifact; returns the new run id | **write — narrow** |
793	| (GraphQL) | `Mutation.runRequeue` | Re-queue the originating bead of an existing run record (manual operator re-queue from the Runs UI); requires `idempotencyKey`; emits a canonical `run_requeue` audit event on the bead (schema below) | **write — narrow** |
794	
795	The HTTP/MCP read surface is total over the unified run substrate.
796	
797	The write surfaces added by this feature are limited to
798	`artifactRegenerate` and `runRequeue`. All other run invocation
799	(layer-1 ad-hoc, layer-2 bead attempts, layer-3 queue drain) remains
800	CLI-only. Additional write endpoints require a separate feature update;
801	they are not implicit in this read-coverage expansion.
802	
803	### Re-queue audit event schema
804	
805	The `runRequeue` mutation reopens the originating bead and appends a
806	`run_requeue` event to that bead's audit log. Concurrent or repeat
807	submissions with the same `idempotencyKey` collapse to a single
808	re-queue and a single event (`deduplicated=true` is returned to all
809	subsequent callers); if the cached idempotency record points at a
810	missing bead, the requeue is replayed against the run's current
811	originating bead. The event uses the standard `bead.BeadEvent`
812	envelope and the following field schema:
813	
814	| Field | Value |
815	|---|---|
816	| `kind` | `run_requeue` (`RunRequeueEventKind`) |
817	| `summary` | `run requeued` |
818	| `actor` | Resolved operator identity; falls back to `anonymous` when no request identity is available |
819	| `source` | `graphql:runRequeue` |
820	| `body` | Single line `key=value` payload: `identity=<identity.kind> actor=<identity.actor> run_id=<runId> idempotency_key=<key> layer_override=<layer-or-empty>` |
821	
822	The body line is structured for grep/jq parsing: each token is a
823	`key=value` pair separated by single spaces. `layer_override` is the
824	empty string when the caller did not pass `RunRequeueInput.layer`.
825	`identity.kind` is the resolver-selected operator identity class
826	(`unknown`, `localhost`, or `tsnet`). `actor` is the resolved user or
827	operator label; when the request carries no identifying header, the
828	audit fields fall back to `identity=unknown actor=anonymous`.
829	
830	The GraphQL mutation response may also report `deduplicated=true`, but
831	that is a write-response flag, not part of the persisted audit event.
832	
833	Consumers of the audit log (FEAT-008 Runs row expansion, evaluation
834	skills under FEAT-019) treat `run_requeue` events as the canonical
835	record that a re-queue happened: there is no separate persisted
836	`requeue` record beyond (a) the new `open` status on the originating
837	bead and (b) this event entry.
838	
839	### Run-detail access audit events
840	
841	The first time the canonical project-scoped run detail is resolved for a
842	run record that is visible through project membership, DDx appends a
843	`run_detail_view` event to the originating bead's audit log. This is the
844	canonical record that a human inspected the run-detail surface.
845	
846	The event is read-side only: it is emitted on the canonical detail load,
847	not on in-page tab switches, evidence downloads, or breadcrumb/back
848	navigation. If the caller cannot see the project, there is no run detail
849	and therefore no audit event.
850	
851	The `body` line is structured for grep/jq parsing and includes the
852	project-scoped identity of the inspection:
853	
854	| Field | Value |
855	|---|---|
856	| `kind` | `run_detail_view` |
857	| `summary` | `run detail viewed` |
858	| `actor` | Viewer identity derived from the authenticated project member |
859	| `source` | `graphql:run` |
860	| `body` | Single line: `project_id=<projectId> run_id=<runId> layer=<layer> visibility=project_membership` |
861	
862	### Layer-to-substrate mapping for the Runs UI
863	
864	The web Runs view (FEAT-008 §5, FEAT-021) renders three layer chips
865	backed by the unified substrate plus the legacy detail backings. The
866	shared run-detail tab strip is the canonical drill-down surface used by
867	FEAT-008, FEAT-019, and FEAT-021:
868	
869	| Chip | Substrate row | Detail backing for row expansion |
870	|---|---|---|
871	| `work` | `layer: 3` Run record | Layer-3 record's queue inputs / stop-condition log / child layer-2 attempt ids; the shared tab strip exposes `overview` only |
872	| `try` | `layer: 2` Run record | The `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundle attached to the layer-2 record (manifest, prompt, result, checks, verdict); the shared tab strip exposes `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `tools`, and `evidence` |
873	| `run` | `layer: 1` Run record | The associated `AgentSession` row (prompt / response / stderr / billing / cached-token detail) joined onto the layer-1 record; the shared tab strip exposes `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `session`, `tools`, and `evidence` |
874	
875	`AgentSession` rows that have no parent layer-2 attempt (raw `ddx
876	agent log` invocations) surface as synthesized `layer=run` Runs rows
877	keyed by session id, so no agent-session row is dropped during the
878	Sessions/Executions tab retirement.
879	
880	The shared tab strip is:
881	
882	- `work` → `overview`
883	- `try` → `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `tools`, `evidence`
884	- `run` → `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `session`, `tools`, `evidence`

886	## User Stories
887	
888	### US-090: Developer Inspects a Layer-1 Run
889	
890	**As a** developer running an ad-hoc agent invocation
891	**I want** to inspect the resulting run record
892	**So that** I can audit prompt, response, tokens, and side-effects
893	
894	**Acceptance Criteria:**
895	- Given I invoke `ddx run`, then DDx writes one layer-1 record under
896	  `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 1` and the layer-1 extension
897	  populated.
898	- Given the run completes, when I run `ddx runs show <run-id>`, then I
899	  see common fields, the layer-1 extension, requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower`,
900	  actual model/power, requested passthrough constraints if supplied, and attachment
901	  references.
902	
903	### US-091: Developer Inspects a Layer-2 Bead Attempt
904	
905	**As a** developer reviewing a bead attempt
906	**I want** to see the attempt's worktree finalization and the child
907	layer-1 invocations
908	**So that** I can audit what the agent did inside the attempt
909	
910	**Acceptance Criteria:**
911	- Given I invoke `ddx try <bead-id>`, then DDx writes one layer-2
912	  record with `layer: 2` referencing one or more layer-1 run ids.
913	- Given the attempt finalizes, when I run `ddx runs show <run-id>`,
914	  then the record reports `merge` or `preserve` and surfaces the
915	  evidence bundle.
916	
917	### US-092: Developer Drains the Queue and Audits the Drain
918	
919	**As a** developer running `ddx work`
920	**I want** the loop to stop on a clearly named condition
921	**So that** I can tell whether the queue was drained, blocked, deferred,
922	spinning, or interrupted
923	
924	**Acceptance Criteria:**
925	- Given I invoke `ddx work`, then DDx writes exactly one layer-3 record
926	  per invocation referencing each child layer-2 attempt id.
927	- Given I press Ctrl-C while `ddx work` or `ddx try` is running, then DDx
928	  prints `Cancel received, shutting down gracefully` before cleanup begins.
929	- Given the loop stops, then the layer-3 record's terminal disposition
930	  is one of `drained`, `blocked`, `deferred`, `no_progress`, `signal`,
931	  and the evaluation log names which condition fired and on which
932	  iteration.
933	
934	### US-093: No-Progress Detection Stops a Spinning Loop
935	
936	**As a** developer running `ddx work`
937	**I want** the loop to stop when consecutive attempts make no progress
938	**So that** I do not burn budget on a pathological queue state
939	
940	**Acceptance Criteria:**
941	- Given `N` consecutive valid implementation attempts produce no commit and no
942	  merged side-effect (default `N = 3`, configurable), then the next
943	  stop-condition evaluation triggers `no_progress` and the layer-3 record
944	  terminates with that disposition.
945	- Given a candidate is skipped by readiness, claim race, routing preflight,
946	  quota/transport/auth/tool setup failure, review error, or operator-action
947	  class, then that iteration does not increment the no-progress counter.
948	- Given retry policy permits escalation before `no_progress` fires, when
949	  a retry is scheduled, then the next layer-1 run raises `MinPower`
950	  and records the actual model/power returned by the agent.
951	- Given the original invocation supplied `--harness`, `--provider`, or
952	  `--model`, then retries pass the same values through unchanged and DDx does
953	  not inspect those values when choosing the next requested `MinPower`.
954	- Given retry escalation would exceed the power available under hard
955	  passthrough pins, then DDx stops with `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or
956	  `agent_power_unsatisfied`, records the Fizeau-supplied evidence, and does not
957	  mutate the pins or call `ResolveRoute`.
958	
959	### US-094: Cross-Layer Run History
960	
961	**As a** workflow tool
962	**I want** to query run history across the task execution layers from one place
963	**So that** I do not have to merge two on-disk shapes by hand
964	
965	**Acceptance Criteria:**
966	- Given `run`, `try`, and `work` records exist, when a tool calls
967	  `GET /api/runs?layer=...`, then it receives records of the requested
968	  layer with consistent common fields.
969	- Given a layer-3 record exists, then its child layer-2 attempt ids and
970	  their child layer-1 run ids are reachable by following
971	  `parent_run_id` pointers.
972	
973	### US-095: Artifact Regeneration Records Provenance
974	
975	**As a** consumer of the document graph
976	**I want** generated artifacts to point back to the run that produced
977	them
978	**So that** staleness can be evaluated against generator inputs
979	
980	**Acceptance Criteria:**
981	- Given I regenerate an artifact via `ddx artifact regenerate <id>`,
982	  then the resulting run record has `produces_artifact: <id>`.
983	- Given a generated artifact has been produced by a run, then
984	  FEAT-007's `generated_by` edge resolves to that run id.
985	
986	### US-096: Operator Migrates From Legacy Run Trees
987	
988	**As a** repo operator adopting the unified substrate
989	**I want** legacy `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`
990	records to remain inspectable during the migration window
991	**So that** adopting `.ddx/runs/` does not strand prior evidence
992	
993	**Acceptance Criteria:**
994	- Given a repository contains legacy run data, when DDx reads run
995	  history, then legacy paths surface through the unified read API as
996	  layer-1 / layer-2 records during the documented migration window.
997	- Given the migration window closes, then writers target only
998	  `.ddx/runs/` and a one-shot rewrite has normalized prior records into
999	  the unified substrate.
1000	
1001	## CONTRACT-003 Amendment
1002	
1003	This feature requires two narrow read-only-amendment-adjacent changes
1004	to CONTRACT-003: write surfaces for `artifactRegenerate` (HTTP/MCP) and
1005	`runRequeue` (GraphQL). `runRequeue` does not invoke the execution service —
1006	it reopens the originating bead so the existing `ddx work` loop can claim it
1007	again — but it is still a write because it mutates bead state and appends an
1008	audit event (see "Re-queue audit event schema" above).
1009	No other write surfaces are added; in particular, layer-1, layer-2,
1010	and layer-3 invocation remain CLI-only.
1011	
1012	If FEAT-006 implementation reveals additional CONTRACT-003 gaps (e.g.,
1013	upstream Cobra root export details), those amendments are scoped to
1014	FEAT-006 and not to this feature.
1015	
1016	## Dependencies
1017	
1018	- FEAT-005 (Artifacts) — `produces_artifact` references artifact IDs
1019	- FEAT-006 (Fizeau consumer contract) — provides the layer-1 consumer-side wrapper
1020	  and CONTRACT-003 boundary
1021	- FEAT-007 (Doc Graph) — `generated_by` edges read `produces_artifact`
1022	  from run records
1023	- DDx CLI infrastructure (config loading, command factory)
1024	
1025	## Out of Scope
1026	
1027	- **Run kinds beyond `run`, `try`, and `work`** — comparison, replay, benchmark,
1028	  adversarial review, effort estimate, and similar workflows are skill
1029	  compositions, not new layers or new on-disk shapes.
1030	- **Content-aware supervisory loop decisions** — `ddx work` performs
1031	  mechanical queue drain only. Decisions like "comparison failed →
1032	  enqueue reconciliation beads" are skill / plugin / workflow-tool
1033	  territory.
1034	- **Server-side layer-1/2/3 invocation** — only `artifactRegenerate`
1035	  has a write endpoint in this feature.
1036	- **Workflow-specific action semantics** — phase routing, methodology
1037	  scoring, tool-specific issue closing — delegated to workflow tools.
1038	- **Workflow-specific autonomy semantics** — DDx owns the mechanical retry,
1039	  review, cooldown, and budget policy in this feature and ADR-024. Higher-level
1040	  methodology decisions such as "file reconciliation beads after a benchmark"
1041	  remain workflow-tool territory.
1042	- **Hosted run history storage** — records are repo-local and
1043	  file-backed.
1044	- **Separate domain-specific runtime stores** — there is one substrate.
1045

10	# ADR-024: Power Escalation and Review Routing Policy
11	
12	**Status:** Accepted
13	**Date:** 2026-05-06
14	**Authors:** bead `ddx-5f1eac4f`
15	
16	## Context
17	
18	DDx now has three overlapping concerns in the bead execution path:
19	
20	- retrying a bead attempt when stronger reasoning could plausibly help
21	- running an adversarial pre-close reviewer gate that is independent from the
22	  implementer
23	- enforcing cost visibility and budget stops without taking ownership of
24	  provider/model routing
25	
26	Fizeau owns harness/provider/model routing. DDx owns bead
27	state, worktree isolation, evidence, gates, review verdicts, cooldowns, and
28	the queue-drain loop. The escalation policy therefore has to be expressed in
29	terms of DDx-owned evidence and abstract power bounds, not concrete model names.
30	
31	Earlier planning used "tier" and profile language. That vocabulary is now
32	migration debt when it implies DDx choosing a provider, model, or fallback chain.
33	The stable contract is `MinPower` / `MaxPower` plus opaque passthrough
34	constraints.
35	
36	## Decision
37	
38	DDx power escalation is a DDx retry-policy decision expressed only by changing
39	the next request's `MinPower` and carrying the same request facts forward.
40	DDx does not choose, substitute, downgrade, or fallback concrete harnesses,
41	providers, or models. Fizeau chooses the concrete route.
42	
43	### Primary Attempt Escalation
44	
45	For a primary bead attempt, DDx may schedule a higher-power retry only when all
46	of the following are true:
47	
48	1. a valid attempt started and produced DDx-owned evidence;
49	2. the outcome is classified as plausibly capability-sensitive; and
50	3. no stop condition, hard passthrough constraint, budget cap, cooldown, or
51	   operator-action class has already taken ownership of the result.
52	
53	Eligible capability-sensitive classes include failed post-run checks,
54	implementation-quality failures, review blocks that identify fixable reasoning
55	or design gaps, and no-changes-after-attempt when the model had valid context.
56	
57	Ineligible classes include routing/passthrough conflicts, missing tools,
58	authentication or quota failures, invalid bead metadata, dirty worktree, hard
59	merge/land conflicts that need human judgment, command-not-found setup failures,
60	and any failure where retry would require DDx to inspect or mutate
61	`--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`.
62	
63	When a higher-power retry is allowed, DDx computes the next `MinPower` floor
64	from Fizeau's catalog power numbers. It may skip catalog power floors that have
65	no viable auto-routable model, but it still sends only a new `MinPower` bound
66	to Fizeau. Fizeau chooses the concrete route and reports actual model,
67	provider, and power.
68	
69	### Passthrough Stickiness
70	
71	Operator-supplied passthrough constraints are sticky across every retry.
72	
73	If the operator supplied `--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`, DDx forwards
74	the same values unchanged. If those constraints make a later power request
75	unsatisfiable, DDx records a typed terminal classification such as
76	`blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or `agent_power_unsatisfied` and reports
77	operator action required. DDx must not remove pins, widen pins, substitute a
78	fallback route, call `ResolveRoute` to work around the conflict, or loop over
79	concrete route names.
80	
81	### Infrastructure And Rate-Limit Fallback
82	
83	Infrastructure failures are not capability failures. Provider 5xx responses,
84	network unreachability, command-not-found, authentication failures, quota
85	exhaustion, and analogous transport/setup failures do not consume escalation
86	budget. DDx records the failure and either leaves the bead immediately
87	reclaimable or applies a retry-after cooldown only when time passing could
88	plausibly fix the same attempt class.
89	
90	HTTP 429 / rate-limit handling is internal to one attempt. The claim stays held,
91	DDx honors a parseable `Retry-After` when present, otherwise uses bounded
92	exponential backoff, and emits `rate-limit-retry` evidence. When the retry
93	budget is exhausted, the attempt falls back to the ordinary `execution_failed`
94	mapping.
95	
96	### Default Adversarial Pre-Close Review Gate
97	
98	Every close-eligible implementation result passes through an adversarial
99	pre-close review gate before DDx mutates the bead to `closed`. Review is enabled
100	by default. Disabling it is an explicit operator override and must record an
101	auditable reason.
102	
103	The gate runs after the implementation attempt has produced stable evidence
104	(`base_rev`, `result_rev`, diff, verification command output, bead metadata, and
105	governing artifacts), and before the durable close mutation. The candidate
106	commit may already exist in git so the reviewer can inspect a stable
107	`result_rev`; the bead is not closed until the review gate approves that result.
108	
109	The default gate dispatches two independent reviewer invocations. Each reviewer
110	runs in TD-033 no-tool reviewer mode over the same bounded evidence bundle and
111	must return a structured verdict with per-acceptance-criterion evidence.
112	
113	DDx requests a reviewer that is stronger than the implementer by using the
114	implementer's actual power as evidence and setting reviewer `MinPower` to a
115	higher floor. DDx also supplies structured correlation facts:
116	
117	- `role=reviewer`
118	- `bead_id`
119	- `attempt_id`
120	- `session_id`
121	- `result_rev`
122	- `review_group_id`
123	- `reviewer_index`
124	- implementer harness/provider/model/power when known
125	
126	These fields are Day-1 observability. DDx records them so operators and metrics
127	can see whether review pairing degraded. Future agent-side routing
128	intelligence may use `Role` and correlation internally, but DDx does not
129	specify that algorithm and must not depend on it.
130	
131	Different-provider review is best effort. If the agent returns a reviewer on
132	the same provider as the implementer, DDx emits a
133	`review-pairing-degraded` event. That event affects triage bias and operator
134	visibility; it is not by itself a review failure.
135	
136	### Review Outcomes And Retry
137	
138	Reviewer output must satisfy the structured verdict contract. A valid approval
139	requires per-AC evidence; an `APPROVE` without evidence is malformed, not a
140	pass. The aggregate gate is conservative:
141	
142	- unanimous reviewer `APPROVE` with per-AC evidence permits close;
143	- any evidenced `REQUEST_CHANGES` or `BLOCK` prevents close;
144	- reviewer disagreement is preserved in the review group evidence, and the
145	  non-approve verdict wins when it cites evidence;
146	- malformed, empty, context-overflow, and transport failures are
147	  `review-error` classes scoped to the reviewed `result_rev` and reviewer.
148	
149	Non-approve reviewer findings are classified before the next action:
150	
151	- `review_fixable_gap` — implementation or test work is missing, incomplete, or
152	  erroneous. DDx schedules a new implementation cycle on the same bead when
153	  retry budgets allow. The next implementation prompt includes the review
154	  findings as required repair context. DDx may raise `MinPower` only when the
155	  finding is plausibly capability-sensitive.
156	- `review_spec_gap` / `review_missing_acceptance` — the bead or governing spec
157	  is ambiguous, unverifiable, contradictory, or missing acceptance criteria. DDx
158	  parks the bead in the `needs_human` operator-attention lane, clears the
159	  active claim, and does not ask another implementer to guess. The operator
160	  resolves that lane by retry, split, obsolete, or defer actions recorded in
161	  the bead history.
162	- `review_too_large` — the result or bead is too broad for bounded review. DDx
163	  runs the intake/decomposition path, blocks or decomposes the parent, and does
164	  not re-run the same monolithic implementation attempt.
165	- `review_unsafe_or_out_of_scope` — the implementation changed forbidden scope,
166	  removed checks, weakened behavior, or otherwise needs explicit repair. If the
167	  repair is mechanical it follows `review_fixable_gap`; otherwise it parks in
168	  `needs_human`.
169	
170	Review errors retry the review path up to `review_max_retries` for the same
171	`result_rev` and reviewer slot. On exhaustion, DDx emits
172	`review-manual-required`, clears the active claim, parks the bead in
173	`needs_human`, and leaves it open for operator review without closing it. This
174	terminal lane resolution is the same contract used by `review_spec_gap` and
175	`review_missing_acceptance`: the bead remains open, carries the human-review
176	context, and waits for an operator decision rather than another automatic
177	implementation attempt. A new implementation result starts a new review-error
178	retry scope, while `review_fixable_gap` continues through the normal repair
179	retry path instead of entering the operator lane.
180	
181	Each implementation/review cycle is append-only. A repair attempt creates a new
182	cycle record linked to the prior review group (`repair_context_from_review_group`)
183	and records its own `base_rev`, `result_rev`, implementer run ids, verification
184	output, reviewer run ids, aggregate verdict, retry/decomposition/block decision,
185	and cost summary. No cycle overwrites prior evidence.
186	
187	### Cost Accounting
188	
189	Primary attempts and review attempts both report usage and cost into the same
190	run evidence stream. FEAT-014 defines the normalized cost signal fields.
191	FEAT-010 owns the queue-drain budget stop. Subscription-bundled and local
192	providers may be excluded from billed-cost caps by explicit cost-class metadata;
193	unknown cost class counts by default.
194	
195	Reviewer cost is not special. When a review invocation reports billable cost,
196	that cost contributes to the same drain-level cap as implementation attempts.
197	If the cap is reached, DDx stops claiming more work and records an observable
198	budget stop instead of disguising the stop as model failure.
199	
200	## Consequences
201	
202	- FEAT-006 owns request construction: `MinPower` / `MaxPower`, passthrough
203	  envelope, role/correlation metadata, and actual power recording.
204	- FEAT-010 owns intake, retry scheduling, stop-condition evaluation,
205	  no-progress handling, adversarial review aggregation, review retry scopes, and
206	  budget stops.
207	- FEAT-014 owns normalized usage, cost, cost-class, and freshness semantics.
208	- FEAT-022 and TD-033 own review evidence assembly and no-tool reviewer mode.
209	- DDx routing lint should treat concrete route mutation in `run`, `try`, or
210	  `work` policy as a regression.
211	
212	## Non-Goals
213	
214	- Specifying Fizeau's internal routing, fallback, provider-health, or model
215	  scoring algorithm.
216	- Guaranteeing a different reviewer provider or model. DDx can request stronger
217	  review, dispatch independent reviewer slots, and record degradation, not force
218	  a route.
219	- Retrying by concrete model name or profile mutation.
220	- Adding a fourth run layer or a review-specific execution substrate.
221	
222	## References
223	
224	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md`
225	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-010-task-execution.md`
226	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-014-token-awareness.md`
