213	## 5. Outcome → Label / Event / Extra Mapping
214	
215	When a drain attempt finishes, `execute-bead` returns one of a fixed set of
216	outcomes plus optional no_changes lifecycle evidence. The mapping below is the
217	canonical queue-management contract.
218	
219	Lifecycle reliability invariants:
220	
221	- Raw `no_changes` is attempt evidence, not a durable bead queue state. The
222	  drain loop MUST translate it into one of the rows below before mutating the
223	  bead.
224	- `execute-loop-retry-after` may be set only when retrying the same bead after
225	  time passes could plausibly succeed without human/spec/dependency changes.
226	- Every bead excluded from ordinary `ddx work` execution MUST have an
227	  explainable durable reason using existing mechanisms: dependency edge,
228	  `blocked` status, `needs_human`/triage labels, `execution-eligible=false`,
229	  `superseded-by`, epic/parent queue mode, or an active retry cooldown.
230	- `closed` means implementation, verification, and the default adversarial
231	  pre-close review gate have all passed, or the work was verified as already
232	  satisfied. Review failure never reopens a closed bead; it prevents close.
233	- Automatic implementation retry is bounded and applies only to classifications
234	  where the implementer had valid task context and further automated work can
235	  plausibly resolve the finding. Spec gaps, missing acceptance criteria,
236	  decomposition overflow, and exhausted reviewer failures require operator
237	  resolution.
238	- Latest terminal events and close evidence beat stale `execute-loop-*` Extra
239	  metadata. Reconciliation may clear stale management fields, but MUST preserve
240	  append-only events and evidence.
241	
242	| Outcome or lifecycle action | Status transition | Label changes | Events appended | Extra updates |
243	|---|---|---|---|---|
244	| `review_pass` after merged candidate | `in_progress → closed` | remove `claimed`, add `last-merged-rev:<sha>` (optional) | `review-pass` + `closed-merged` | `Extra["last-run"]`, `Extra["last-review"]`, and `Extra["closing_commit_sha"]` updated; clear stale `execute-loop-*` management fields |
245	| `already_satisfied` | `in_progress → closed` | (none) | `closed-already-satisfied` | `Extra["last-run"]`; clear stale `execute-loop-*` management fields |
246	| `review_fixable_gap` with retry budget remaining | no durable status change if continuing immediately; otherwise `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add no human label unless retry budget exhausted | `review-fixable-gap` + retry decision | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref; next cycle records `repair_context_from_review_group` |
247	| `review_fixable_gap` with retry budget exhausted | `in_progress → blocked` | add `needs_human` | `review-block` | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref and exhausted budget |
248	| `review_spec_gap` / `review_missing_acceptance` | `in_progress → blocked` | add `needs_human`, `triage:spec-gap` or `triage:missing-acceptance` | `review-block` | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref |
249	| `review_too_large` | `in_progress → blocked` after children are filed, or `in_progress → blocked` with `needs-human-decomposition` at depth cap | add `decomposed` when children exist; otherwise add `needs_human` | `review-too-large` and optionally `triage-decomposed` / `triage-overflow` | `Extra["children"]` and AC mapping when decomposed |
250	| `review_error` below retry cap | no durable status change; retry reviewer for same `result_rev` | (none) | `review-error` | `Extra["last-review-error"]` carries class, reviewer slot, and attempt count |
251	| `review_error` exhausted | `in_progress → blocked` | add `needs_human` | `review-manual-required` | `Extra["last-review-error"]` carries class, reviewer slot, and attempt count |
252	| `execution_failed` | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | (none) | `unclaimed` + a structured failure event | `Extra["last-run"]` |
253	| verified no_changes already satisfied | `in_progress → closed` | remove no_changes triage labels if present | `no_changes_verified` + `closed-already-satisfied` | `Extra["last-run"]`; clear `execute-loop-retry-after`, `execute-loop-last-status`, and `execute-loop-last-detail` |
254	| unverified no_changes | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add `triage:no-changes-unverified` | `no_changes_unverified` | record verification command/result; do not set retry cooldown by default |
255	| unjustified no_changes / no rationale | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add `triage:no-changes-unjustified` | `no_changes_unjustified` | record rationale absence/detail; do not set retry cooldown by default |
256	| no_changes needs investigation because work is too large | `in_progress → blocked` after orchestrator child beads are filed, or `in_progress → blocked` with `needs-human-decomposition` at queue-level depth cap | add `decomposed` when children exist; add `needs_human` only for lossy/ambiguous/depth-overflow cases | `no_changes_needs_investigation` + `triage-decomposed` or `triage-overflow` | `Extra["last-rationale"]`, `Extra["children"]`, and AC mapping; do not set retry cooldown |
257	| no_changes needs investigation for non-decomposition reason | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add `triage:needs-investigation`; add `needs_human` when operator action is required before retry | `no_changes_needs_investigation` | `Extra["last-rationale"]`; do not set retry cooldown by default |
258	| parent/epic/decomposed container | `in_progress → blocked` when children were created; otherwise `in_progress → open` with `execution-eligible=false` | add `decomposed` when children exist | `no_changes_decomposed` or `triage-decomposed` | `Extra["children"]` lists child IDs plus AC mapping, or `Extra["execution-eligible"]=false` explains container-only work |
259	| external blocker | `in_progress → blocked` for hard blockers, or `in_progress → open` with a `blocked-on-upstream:<id>` label for visible soft blockers | add `needs_human` or `blocked-on-upstream:<id>` | `no_changes_blocked` | `Extra["last-rationale"]` names the external blocker |
260	| superseded work | no terminal success; leave open only if visible history is needed | add no triage labels | structured superseded event if appended by caller | `Extra["superseded-by"]` names the replacement and makes ordinary execution ineligible |
261	| transient infra/quota/transport | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | (none) | `no_changes_recoverable`, `drain-paused-quota`, `rate-limit-retry`, or structured transport event | may set `execute-loop-retry-after` only for the retryable time-based condition |
262	| stale no_changes tracker metadata | no status change unless latest terminal evidence closes the bead | remove stale no_changes triage labels only when contradicted by terminal evidence | preserve historical events; append reconciliation event if performed | clear stale `execute-loop-*` management fields when latest terminal event or close evidence proves they are obsolete |
263	
264	`needs_human` is a **label**, not a status. It is the standard signal that
265	a human operator should look at the bead before drain re-attempts it.

267	## 6. Claim Semantics
268	
269	Claim is metadata, not status. The persisted-status convention is that
270	`in_progress` implies a claim is held; `open` implies no claim. The actual
271	claim fields are:
272	
273	- `Extra["assignee"]` (string) — the entity holding the claim.
274	- `Extra["claimed-at"]` (RFC3339 timestamp) — when the claim was acquired.
275	- `Extra["claimed-pid"]` (int) — process id of the claim holder, advisory.
276	
277	Acquired:
278	
279	- During `open → in_progress` transition under the store lock.

50	- Two on-disk record shapes for fundamentally similar artifacts (a single
51	  agent invocation, with logs, structured result, and provenance)
52	- No first-class concept of the task execution layers (`run` / `try` /
53	  `work`), so consumers must intuit which surface to read
54	- Stop conditions for queue drain are implicit and not observable
55	- Generated-artifact provenance has no clean hook into the run record
56	- Open-ended catalog pressure: every new "run flavor" (benchmark, replay,
57	  comparison) tempts a new on-disk shape or a new top-level verb
58	
59	**Desired outcome:** A single on-disk run substrate with explicit layer
60	metadata, three top-level CLI verbs that map 1:1 onto the layers, and a
61	narrow read-only HTTP/MCP write surface limited to artifact regeneration.
62	
63	## Task Execution Layers
64	
65	### Layer 1 — `ddx run` (invocation atom)
66	
67	A layer-1 run is one AI invocation. Inputs are a prompt, requested
68	`MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`, optional operator passthrough constraints,
69	and non-routing execution config; outputs are the structured response (text or
70	bytes), any side-effects the service performed via tools, and run metadata
71	(tokens, model, actual power, duration, exit status, session pointer).
72	
73	Layer 1 calls the upstream Fizeau execution contract directly. DDx does not
74	reimplement routing or the invocation loop; it wraps one `Execute` call with
75	provenance capture.
76	
77	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is layer 1 when the generator returns
78	bytes/text and DDx writes the file; it is layer 2 when the generator
79	edits the repo directly (bead-less worktree).
80	
81	### Layer 2 — `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt)
82	
83	A layer-2 run is one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. It owns:
84	
85	- Worktree creation from a base revision and worktree finalization
86	  (merge or preserve)
87	- Worktree cleanup after finalization, failed setup, failed publish, and
88	  ordinary interruption
89	- Bead → prompt resolution (description, acceptance, governing artifacts)
90	- Side-effect bundling (commits, evidence, no-changes rationale)
91	- One or more layer-1 invocations recorded inside the attempt record
92	
93	A layer-2 record references its child layer-1 records by run id.
94	
95	Layer 2 owns bead-attempt success classification. DDx determines success from
96	the artifacts it owns: commit presence, merge/preserve result, no-changes
97	rationale, post-run checks, review verdicts, and cooldown policy. The agent's
98	exit status and actual model/power are inputs to that decision, not the whole
99	decision.
100	
101	Layer 2 records raw attempt evidence. Layer 3 applies
102	[`TD-031`](../../02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md) when
103	that evidence requires durable bead action such as close-as-already-satisfied,
104	human triage, blocked, retry cooldown, or stale no_changes metadata cleanup.
105	
106	### Quality hooks
107	
108	ADR-023 defines two lifecycle quality hooks owned by the layer-2 and layer-3
109	execution loop. They are hooks inside `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, not new run
110	layers and not new bead-schema fields.
111	
112	`BeadReadinessHook` is the product concept: it runs after the worker has
113	selected a dependency-ready candidate but before DDx claims the bead or
114	creates the implementation worktree. The canonical decision being made is bead
115	readiness assessment, the pre-claim check for tractability and actionability;
116	lint/rubric scoring is the diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt
117	triage is a separate after-evidence action.
118	The implementation may still call the compatibility entrypoint
119	`PreClaimIntakeHook` and record `MODE: intake` for legacy compatibility, but
120	those names are aliases only. Within readiness, the nested bead-lifecycle
121	workflow skill performs lint/rubric scoring to support the decision, and the
122	score is diagnostic evidence rather than a separate queue action. Post-attempt
123	triage is separate and runs only after evidence exists. The hook has enough
124	context to evaluate title, description, acceptance criteria, labels, parent,
125	deps, bead type, spec-id, prior attempt history, and whether the bead is
126	atomic enough to execute. The hook invokes the nested bead-lifecycle workflow
127	skill under the `ddx` skill tree and records readiness evidence in the
128	layer-3 run record.
129	
130	`ddx work` wires this hook by default in both CLI and server-managed worker
131	paths. Decomposition decisions run with a strong `MinPower` floor, defaulting to
132	the smart/top-power tier floor when no project-specific splitter override is
133	configured. DDx does not choose the concrete model; it passes the raised power
134	floor to Fizeau and preserves any operator-supplied harness/provider/model
135	passthrough constraints. If those constraints cannot satisfy the strong floor,
136	DDx records `agent_power_unsatisfied` and blocks instead of running weak
137	decomposition.
138	
139	The readiness assessment result is one of:
140	
141	- `actionable_atomic` — claim and execute normally.
142	- `actionable_but_rewritten` — DDx applied safe, intent-preserving bead updates
143	  such as formatting the description into the authoring template, adding
144	  discovered file:line evidence, adding an obvious test command, or wiring
145	  deterministic labels/parent/deps. The update is recorded through `ddx bead`
146	  mutation paths before claim.
147	- `too_large_decomposed` — DDx created child beads, mapped every parent AC to
148	  child ACs or an explicit `needs_human` / `non_scope` marker, blocked the
149	  parent, and did not execute the parent.
150	- `ambiguous_needs_human` — the bead/spec is unclear, contradictory,
151	  unverifiable, or missing acceptance criteria that DDx cannot safely invent.
152	  DDx blocks or marks the bead `needs_human` and does not claim it.
153	- `readiness_error` — readiness infrastructure failed. In migration/WARN mode
154	  this fails open with evidence; in reliable factory/BLOCK mode it may skip the
155	  candidate for the current pass but must not park it behind a cooldown unless a
156	  retryable time-based class is recorded.
157	
158	Safe rewrites may clarify existing intent; they must not invent product
159	behavior, choose between conflicting requirements, change scope, or guess a
160	missing governing artifact. Those cases become `ambiguous_needs_human`.
161	
162	`PostAttemptTriageHook` runs after the attempt has produced its local evidence:
163	agent result, commit/no-commit state, no-changes rationale if any, post-run
164	checks, adversarial review verdict, merge/preserve result, and the readiness
165	report.
166	It uses the same bead-lifecycle skill to classify whether the outcome is a
167	normal attempt result, a quality-policy failure, a missing-evidence failure, or
168	an infrastructure failure. This is distinct from bead readiness assessment and
169	from the lint/rubric scoring used inside readiness. The hook must never rewrite
170	the attempt result or erase artifacts; it only adds triage evidence and feeds
171	retry/stop classification.
172	
173	If post-attempt triage finds that an implementation attempt stopped because the
174	bead was too large or the worker could not legally decompose inside its
175	worktree/depth context, the layer-3 worker must invoke the same orchestrator
176	decomposition path used by `BeadReadinessHook`. This is machine-actionable
177	work: DDx files child beads, records the AC map, and blocks the parent unless
178	the split is lossy, ambiguous, or at the queue-level decomposition depth cap.
179	The operator is not required merely because the implementer could not split
180	from inside the attempted execution.
181	
182	`ExecuteBeadReport` gains `OutcomeReason` beside the existing `Disrupted`
183	signal. `Disrupted` remains the mechanical indicator that normal completion was
184	interrupted. `OutcomeReason` is the stable classification string that explains
185	why the attempt ended, such as `lint_warn`, `lint_blocked`,
186	`lint_hook_error_fail_open`, `readiness_ambiguous`, `readiness_decomposed`,
187	`triage_missing_evidence`, `review_fixable_gap`, `review_spec_gap`,
188	`review_too_large`, `post_run_check_failed`, or `success`. Layer-3 `ddx work`
189	records aggregate these reasons when evaluating retry eligibility, no-progress,
190	blocked, and deferred stop conditions.
191	
192	The hook evidence lives in the attempt evidence bundle and is surfaced through
193	the unified run substrate. The originating bead may receive an evidence event
194	only for explicit operator override (`--force --reason`) or other existing
195	audit events; lint scores are not copied into `.ddx/beads.jsonl`.
196	
197	### Layer 3 — `ddx work` (queue drain)
198	
199	A layer-3 run is one drain of the bead queue. It iterates `ddx try`
200	across ready beads until a stop condition is met. It owns:
201	
202	- Queue iteration order
203	- Pre-claim readiness, safe bead improvement, decomposition, claim acquisition,
204	  claim release, and shutdown/interruption cleanup for claimed beads, using the
205	  TD-031 claim-state contract
206	- Durable bead action after each layer-2 attempt, using TD-031's outcome,
207	  no_changes, cooldown, and stale-metadata rules
208	- No-progress / stop-condition evaluation
209	- A loop-level record that references its child layer-2 records by
210	  attempt id and reports terminal disposition (drained, blocked,
211	  deferred, no-progress)
212	
213	Content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue
214	reconciliation beads") are not layer-3 concerns. Those are skill or
215	plugin compositions on top of the task execution layers.
216	
217	Layer 3 treats raw attempt evidence separately from durable bead action. When a
218	worker stops, receives SIGTERM/SIGINT, loses its child agent process, or honors
219	operator cancel before a terminal bead mutation, it follows TD-031: preserve
220	evidence, record the interruption/disruption, release the claim, and leave the
221	bead re-claimable unless an explicit retryable cooldown or blocker was recorded.
222	Shutdown is not itself a reason to park a bead on `execute-loop-retry-after`.
223	
224	## Agent Power and Retry
225	
226	DDx owns retry policy between bead attempts. If an attempt produces classified
227	evidence that more capable reasoning could plausibly change the result, DDx may
228	retry the bead with a higher `MinPower`.
229	
230	Fizeau owns routing within the requested power bounds. A retry request says
231	"use at least this much power" by raising `MinPower`; an explicit `MaxPower`, if
232	supplied, remains a hard upper bound. Operator-supplied `--harness`,
233	`--provider`, and `--model` values may be sent to Fizeau as passthrough
234	constraints, but DDx does not interpret them. Each layer-1 record stores:
235	
236	- requested `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`
237	- requested passthrough constraints, if any
238	- actual model and actual power reported by Fizeau
239	- run outcome and DDx attempt outcome
240	
241	DDx may query Fizeau's available model/power catalog to choose escalation
242	targets such as "retry using only top-power models." DDx uses the returned power
243	numbers only to compute `MinPower` thresholds; Fizeau still chooses the
244	concrete model.
245	
246	Passthrough constraints stay sticky across retries. DDx may increase `MinPower`
247	between attempts, but it must not alter or drop operator-supplied
248	harness/provider/model passthrough values to make escalation work. If the
249	combination is invalid or constraining, the agent owns that typed error or
250	actual route. DDx stops with `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or
251	`agent_power_unsatisfied`, records the evidence, and reports operator action
252	required; it does not remove pins, widen pins, call `ResolveRoute`, or retry in a
253	loop.
254	
255	### Retry eligibility
256	
257	Power retry is eligible only when DDx-owned evidence shows that a stronger model
258	could plausibly help after a valid attempt started. Eligible classes include:
259	
260	- `capability_insufficient` — the agent attempted the bead, but reasoning
261	  quality or implementation quality was insufficient.
262	- `post_run_check_failed` — tests or gates failed after a valid checkout and
263	  attempted change.
264	- `review_fixable_gap` — adversarial review found implementation or test gaps
265	  plausibly addressable by another automated cycle.
266	- `no_changes_after_attempt` — the agent had a valid checkout and task context
267	  but produced no usable change, subject to the no-progress budget.
268	
269	Power retry is not eligible for deterministic setup or operator-action
270	failures:
271	
272	- dirty worktree, merge/land conflict, missing checkout, invalid bead metadata,
273	  unresolved dependencies, config parse errors, missing harness binaries,
274	  authentication failures, command-not-found/toolchain setup failures
275	- readiness ambiguity, missing acceptance criteria, spec contradictions,
276	  decomposition overflow, review errors, reviewer context overflow, claim races,
277	  routing preflight rejection, quota/transport disruption, and any other class
278	  where the implementer did not receive valid task context and an opportunity to
279	  act
280	- `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` / `agent_power_unsatisfied`
281	- any status where retry would require DDx to inspect, remove, rewrite, or
282	  substitute `--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`
283	
284	Every retry/stop decision records the classification and evidence used. The
285	policy may inspect DDx attempt outcomes and the agent's typed status; it must
286	not branch on concrete provider/model identity.
287	
288	ADR-024 is the decision record for this policy. FEAT-010 owns when `ddx try` or
289	`ddx work` schedules a retry, how no-progress and stop conditions are recorded,
290	how review-error retry scopes are counted, and how budget caps stop a drain.
291	FEAT-006 owns only the agent request envelope used by those decisions.
292	
293	### Execution Cycle Traceability
294	
295	A bead execution is an append-only chain of implementation/review cycles. Layer
296	3 owns the chain; layer 2 owns the evidence for each cycle. A repair attempt
297	must create a new cycle record rather than overwrite the rejected one.
298	
299	Each cycle records:
300	
301	- `cycle_index`
302	- `attempt_id`
303	- `base_rev`
304	- `result_rev` when a stable candidate exists
305	- implementer layer-1 run ids, requested power bounds, actual route/power, and
306	  cost/usage facts
307	- verification command output and exit status
308	- `review_group_id`, reviewer layer-1 run ids, reviewer requested/actual
309	  route facts, aggregate verdict, per-AC evidence summary, and reviewer cost
310	- prior review linkage (`repair_context_from_review_group`) when this cycle is
311	  a repair attempt
312	- final cycle decision: close, retry, decompose, block, manual-required, or
313	  budget stop
314	
315	When a review requires another implementation cycle, the next implementation
316	prompt includes the prior review group's findings as required repair context.
317	The prior cycle remains immutable so operators can answer what changed, why the
318	first result was rejected, and why the final result passed.
319	
320	### Escalation, fallback, retry, and review decision tree
321	
322	The layer-3 drain evaluates each ready bead through this mechanical sequence:
323	
324	1. **Eligibility and readiness.** Pick a dependency-ready candidate. Run the
325	   readiness gate. Safe rewrites happen before claim. Too-large work
326	   is decomposed before an implementation attempt. Ambiguous or underspecified
327	   work is blocked with `needs_human`. Readiness infrastructure failure records
328	   evidence and follows the configured fail-open/factory-mode policy; it never
329	   creates an unexplained cooldown.

9	# Technical Design: Bead State Machine and Naming-Role Decisions
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This TD is the canonical reference for what bead state means in DDx and where
14	each observed name in the codebase belongs. It exists because schema, docs,
15	code, and persisted data have drifted: the bead-record JSON Schema enumerates
16	six statuses (the bd/br canonical set), FEAT-004 still documents three, and
17	several additional names appear in code paths (`done`, `needs_human`,
18	`pending`, `ready`, `review`, `needs_investigation`) without a single decision
19	record explaining whether each is a status, a derived queue category, an
20	event kind, a label, or worker state.
21	
22	The five in-flight hygiene beads each introduce new state-machine vocabulary:
23	
24	- ddx-b24e9630 — `no_changes_*` outcome verification (NoChangesContract)
25	- ddx-3c154349 — auto-triage of stuck beads (TriageContract)
26	- ddx-aede917d — drain pause on quota exhaustion (QuotaPauseContract)
27	- ddx-c6e3db02 — rate-limit retry behavior (RateLimitRetryContract)
28	- ddx-da11a34a — store-lock contention handling (LockContentionContract)
29	
30	Without one TD that nails down the categories and the transition matrix,
31	those five beads will each independently invent overlapping vocabulary.
32	
33	This TD authors the design only. The mechanical reconciliation
34	(schema-vs-FEAT-004 alignment, code rename of non-status names, CI guards,
35	hygiene-bead AC substitution, migration survey) is filed as sibling beads.
36	
37	## Critical Constraint (per ADR-004)
38	
39	The bead-record envelope must remain compatible with the bd/br interchange
40	contract. The persisted `status` enum is **fixed** at the bd/br canonical six
41	values:
42	
43	```
44	open, in_progress, closed, blocked, proposed, cancelled
45	```
46	
47	DDx-specific execution semantics live in **labels**, **events**, or the
48	preserved-extras **Extra** map — never in new statuses. Adding a new persisted
49	status requires upstream bd/br coordination plus an ADR-004 amendment. This
50	TD does not authorize any such addition.
51	
52	## Today's Observed State (verified)
53	
54	- `cli/internal/bead/schema/bead-record.schema.json` enumerates six values.
55	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-004-beads.md` line 65 still documents
56	  three values (`open, in_progress, closed`). FEAT-004 lags the schema.
57	- `ddx bead list --json | jq -r '.[].status' | sort -u` on real projects
58	  returns only `open`, `in_progress`, `closed` for active rows today —
59	  `blocked`, `proposed`, and `cancelled` are schema-allowed but unused in
60	  the current active queue.
61	- The names `done`, `needs_human`, `pending`, `ready`, `review`,
62	  `needs_investigation` appear in code paths but are **not** persisted bead
63	  statuses today. Each falls into one of the other categories defined below.
64	
65	## Sections
66	
67	The remainder of this TD is structured as the eleven design sections required
68	by the originating bead, followed by five per-hygiene-bead contract
69	subsections.
70	
71	---
72	
73	## 1. Category Taxonomy
74	
75	Every state-machine name observed in DDx falls into exactly one of the
76	following categories. The category determines where the name is allowed to
77	live (schema, label namespace, event kind, queue derivation, or worker
78	process state).
79	
80	| Category | Storage location | Lifecycle | Owner |
81	|---|---|---|---|
82	| Persisted bead status | `status` field on the bead record | Mutated by atomic snapshot rewrite | Bead store, locked to bd/br set |
83	| Derived queue category | Computed on read from status + labels + deps | Never persisted | Queue derivation code (`ddx bead ready/blocked/status`) |
84	| Event kind | Append-only entry in `Extra["events"][].kind` | Append-only | Drain loop, agent service, CLI |
85	| Terminal phase | A persisted `closed` status plus a closing event/label that names *why* | Mutated once on close | Drain loop / CLI |
86	| Claim metadata | `assignee`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid` fields (preserved extras) | Set on claim, cleared on unclaim, expired by triage | Claim resolution path (TD-004) |
87	| Label | Entry in the `labels` array | Add/remove during normal mutation | Anyone with `ddx bead update` |
88	| Extra metadata field | Arbitrary key under preserved extras | Free-form per consumer | Subsystem owning that key |
89	| Worker state | In-memory state of the drain process | Lives only for the worker's lifetime | Drain loop process |
90	
91	A name MUST NOT span categories. If a name today appears as both a label and
92	a derived queue category (e.g., `ready` is derived; `ready` MUST NOT also
93	appear as a label), the sibling reconciliation bead removes the duplicate
94	usage.
95	
96	## 2. Persisted Status Enumeration
97	
98	The persisted bead status is exactly:
99	
100	```
101	open | in_progress | closed | blocked | proposed | cancelled
102	```
103	
104	This is the bd/br-canonical set; it matches `bead-record.schema.json`. This
105	TD does **not** add, rename, or remove any persisted status.
106	
107	Plain-English semantics:
108	
109	- `open` — the bead exists and is eligible for queue derivation. No claim is
110	  active.
111	- `in_progress` — the bead has an active claim (`assignee`, `claimed-at`,
112	  `claimed-pid` populated). The drain loop or an operator has taken
113	  ownership.
114	- `closed` — the bead is terminal. The reason for closure is encoded in a
115	  closing event and/or label; the status itself does not carry the reason.
116	- `blocked` — the bead cannot progress because of a hard precondition (an
117	  upstream dependency, an external blocker). The reason is encoded in a
118	  label (`blocked-on-upstream:<id>`) or an event.
119	- `proposed` — the bead is captured but has not been accepted into the
120	  active queue. Used for triage backlogs; not consumed by drain.
121	- `cancelled` — the bead was abandoned without completion. Distinct from
122	  `closed`: `cancelled` means "we are not doing this work"; `closed` means
123	  "the work is done (or determined unnecessary)".
124	
125	## 3. Transition Matrix
126	
127	| From → To | Allowed? | Driver | Event fired |
128	|---|---|---|---|
129	| `proposed` → `open` | yes | operator (`ddx bead update --status open`) | `triaged` |
130	| `proposed` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
131	| `open` → `in_progress` | yes | drain loop or operator (claim) | `claimed` |
132	| `open` → `blocked` | yes | operator or auto-triage | `blocked` |
133	| `open` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
134	| `in_progress` → `open` | yes | unclaim (operator or stale-claim sweep) | `unclaimed` |
135	| `in_progress` → `closed` | yes | drain loop (on merge/already-satisfied) or operator | `closed-merged` / `closed-already-satisfied` |
136	| `in_progress` → `blocked` | yes | drain loop on non-automatable review/readiness finding, or operator | `review-block` / `blocked` |
137	| `blocked` → `open` | yes | operator (block resolved) | `unblocked` |
138	| `blocked` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
139	| `closed` → * | no | — | — (closed is terminal) |
140	| `cancelled` → * | no | — | — (cancelled is terminal) |
141	| any → `proposed` | no | — | proposed is an entry-only status |
142	
143	Closed and cancelled are terminal. Re-opening a closed bead is not a
144	transition; it is filing a follow-up bead with `replaces` set.
145	
146	## 4. Event Vocabulary (unified)
147	
148	Events are append-only entries on `Extra["events"]` (see TD-004 evidence
149	model). The kinds below form the closed vocabulary used across the five
150	hygiene beads. New kinds require updating this section.
151	
152	Lifecycle events:
153	
154	- `triaged` — `proposed → open`
155	- `claimed` — claim acquired
156	- `unclaimed` — claim released
157	- `blocked` — moved to `blocked` (carries a reason in `body`)
158	- `unblocked` — moved off `blocked`
159	- `closed-merged` — drain loop closed after a merge
160	- `closed-already-satisfied` — drain loop detected the work was already done
161	- `closed` — operator-driven close (catch-all)
162	- `cancelled` — moved to `cancelled`
163	
164	Drain-outcome events (no_changes family — NoChangesContract):
165	
166	- `no_changes_verified` — the attempt produced no commit, but supplied a
167	  `verification_command` that passed; the bead is already satisfied.
168	- `no_changes_unverified` — the attempt produced no commit and supplied a
169	  `verification_command`, but the command failed or could not run.
170	- `no_changes_unjustified` — the attempt produced no commit without enough
171	  structured rationale to prove either satisfaction or a durable blocker.
172	- `no_changes_needs_investigation` — the attempt explicitly asked for operator
173	  triage with `status: needs_investigation`.
174	- `no_changes_decomposed` — agent decomposed the bead instead of changing
175	  files.
176	- `no_changes_blocked` — agent declared no_changes with a justified external
177	  blocker.
178	- `no_changes_recoverable` — transient cause; retrying the same bead after
179	  time passes can plausibly succeed.
180	
181	Drain-control events:
182	
183	- `drain-paused-quota` — QuotaPauseContract: drain paused because the active
184	  harness reported quota exhaustion
185	- `drain-resumed-quota`
186	- `rate-limit-retry` — RateLimitRetryContract: a single attempt was retried
187	  after a rate-limit response
188	- `lock-contention` — LockContentionContract: store-lock contention was
189	  observed and handled (retry or backoff)
190	
191	Review and triage events:
192	
193	- `review-block` — reviewer raised a BLOCKING finding
194	- `review-pass` — reviewer cleared the change
195	- `review-fixable-gap` — reviewer found an implementation/test gap that can be
196	  repaired by another automated cycle
197	- `review-too-large` — reviewer or readiness found the bead/result too broad for a
198	  bounded review cycle
199	- `review-error` — reviewer invocation failed, was malformed, overflowed, or
200	  returned no parseable verdict

280	- The drain loop and operator CLI (`ddx bead update --claim`) are the two
281	  legitimate drivers.
282	
283	Released:
284	
285	- Explicit `--unclaim` (or implicit unclaim on `in_progress → open`).
286	- On terminal close (`closed` / `cancelled`) the claim metadata is left in
287	  place as historical record but is no longer authoritative.
288	
289	Worker shutdown and interruption:
290	
291	- A worker that receives graceful shutdown while no bead attempt has reached a
292	  terminal mutation MUST release any active claim it owns before exiting. The
293	  bead returns `in_progress → open`, claim metadata is cleared, and an
294	  `unclaimed` event records `reason=worker_shutdown`.
295	- If the worker has already applied a terminal mutation (`closed`,
296	  `cancelled`, or `blocked`) for the current attempt, shutdown MUST NOT undo
297	  that terminal state. The worker may emit best-effort worker-disconnect
298	  telemetry, but bead state is already authoritative.
299	- If the child agent process or attempt worktree is interrupted by context
300	  cancellation, SIGTERM, SIGINT, or a server/operator cancel before a terminal
301	  mutation, the attempt is classified as mechanically disrupted. The worker
302	  preserves any available evidence, appends a structured attempt/interruption
303	  event, releases the claim, and leaves the bead re-claimable unless a separate
304	  explicit blocker or retryable cooldown was recorded.
305	- An ungraceful worker death may strand an `in_progress` bead temporarily. The
306	  stale-claim sweep is the recovery path: after the configured stale threshold,
307	  it releases the claim, appends the recovery event, and makes the bead
308	  queue-eligible again. This recovery MUST NOT delete the original attempt
309	  events or evidence bundle.
310	- Cooldown is not a shutdown-cleanup mechanism. A stopped or interrupted worker
311	  may set `execute-loop-retry-after` only when the recorded outcome is a
312	  retryable time-based condition; ordinary shutdown, SIGTERM/SIGINT, or
313	  operator cancel releases the claim without parking the bead behind time.
314	
315	Stale claim handling (TriageContract owns the policy):
316	
317	- A claim is *stale* if `claimed-at` is older than the configured stale
318	  threshold AND the bead is still `in_progress`.
319	- Stale claims are released by the auto-triage path (`auto-triage` event),
320	  status moves `in_progress → open`, and the bead becomes queue-eligible
321	  again.
322	- Auto-triage MUST NOT delete prior claim metadata; it appends an event
323	  recording the release.
324	
325	## 7. Naming-Role Decision Matrix
326	
327	Every name observed in code, schema, docs, or persisted data is assigned
328	a single category here. Names not in the persisted-status set MUST NOT
329	appear as `status` values.
330	
331	| Name | Category | Rationale |
332	|---|---|---|
333	| `open` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; queue-eligible default. |
334	| `in_progress` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; implies an active claim. |
335	| `closed` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; terminal-success path. |
336	| `blocked` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; hard precondition unmet. |
337	| `proposed` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; pre-triage backlog. |
338	| `cancelled` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; terminal-abandoned path. |
339	| `done` | Removed alias | Historical alias of `closed` in code paths. Not a persisted status. The reconciliation sibling bead replaces remaining code references with `closed`. |
340	| `pending` | Derived queue category | Used by queue derivation to mean "open AND has unmet deps" (a synonym for unsatisfied dependencies). Never persisted. The reconciliation sibling bead either renames it to `waiting` or folds it into `blocked` derivation. |
341	| `ready` | Derived queue category | "open AND no unmet deps AND not claimed". Computed from status+deps+claim; never persisted. |
342	| `review` | Terminal phase / event | A *phase of work*, not a status. Implemented as the `review-block` / `review-pass` event pair plus the `needs_human` label. Never persisted as a status. |
343	| `needs_human` | Label | Signals "an operator must intervene". Stays a label per bd/br compatibility — never a status. |
344	| `needs_investigation` | Label | Signals "the cause is unclear; triage required". Stays a **label** per bd/br compatibility. The reconciliation sibling bead removes any code path that treats it as a status. |
345	| `blocked-on-upstream:<id>` | Label | Parameterized label naming the upstream blocker. Distinct from the `blocked` status: a bead can carry the label while remaining `open` for visibility, or while `blocked` for enforcement. |
346	| `decomposed` | Label | Set by drain on `no_changes_decomposed`; pairs with `Extra["children"]`. |
347	| `triage` | Label | Set by drain on `no_changes_no_evidence` or by auto-triage. |
348	| `idle` / `draining` / `paused-quota` / `paused-rate-limit` / `exiting` | Worker state | Lives in the drain loop process, not on the bead. See section 10. |
349	
350	## 8. Per-Hygiene-Bead Contracts
351	
352	Each hygiene bead's AC must cite this section. Anything not in the
353	contract here is out of scope for that bead.
354	
355	### 8.1 NoChangesContract (ddx-b24e9630)
356	
357	NoChangesContract outcomes use the canonical mapping in section 5. This section
358	exists only to bind the hygiene bead to that mapping; it does not define a
359	second disposition table.
360	
361	Claim behavior: the claim is released for every no_changes action that leaves
362	the bead `open`. It is not released separately when the same mutation moves the
363	bead directly to terminal `closed` or hard `blocked`.
364	
365	Loop interaction: the try package parses and verifies no_changes rationale,
366	then returns the lifecycle action. The drain loop applies section 5 exactly and
367	appends one of the no_changes event kinds from section 4; it does not invent
368	new event kinds or use cooldown as a generic parking lot.
369	
370	### 8.2 Bead Readiness Assessment And Triage Contract (ddx-3c154349)
371	
372	Bead readiness assessment is the canonical pre-claim decision for
373	actionability and scope. It owns the readiness queue decision and runs before a
374	worker owns the bead, so most readiness actions start from `open`.
375	Lint/rubric scoring is the diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt
376	triage is a separate after-evidence queue action. The implementation entrypoint
377	may still be named `MODE: intake` for compatibility, but that is legacy
378	wording only; the product concept is bead readiness assessment.
379	
380	Status transitions used:
381	
382	- `in_progress → open` when releasing stale claims.
383	- `open → blocked` when triage decides a bead has an unmet hard
384	  precondition.
385	- `open → blocked` when readiness decomposes a parent, reaches decomposition depth
386	  overflow, or finds ambiguity that cannot be safely rewritten.
387	
388	Labels added: `triage`, `needs_human`, `blocked-on-upstream:<id>`,
389	`decomposed`, `needs-human-decomposition`, `triage:spec-gap`,
390	`triage:missing-acceptance` (as applicable).
391	
392	Labels removed: `triage` after a triaged bead is reclaimed cleanly (the
393	reverse mutation of `triage` is "next successful claim removes the
394	label").
395	
396	Events fired: `auto-triage`, `triage-ambiguous`, `triage-decomposed`,
397	`triage-overflow`, `unclaimed`, optionally `blocked`.
398	
399	Claim behavior: the auto-triage sweep is the only path that releases a
400	claim it does not own. It MUST log the prior `assignee` and `claimed-at`
401	in the appended event body.
402	
403	Loop interaction: readiness and triage run out-of-band of execute-bead. They
404	MUST NOT race with an active drain attempt holding the store lock — they
405	acquire the same store lock and releases-then-reclaims is not their job.
406	
407	### 8.3 QuotaPauseContract (ddx-aede917d)
408	
409	Status transitions used: none. Quota is a worker-state concern, not a
410	bead-state concern.
411	
412	Labels: none added on the affected bead. The current attempt is treated
413	as `execution_failed` (claim released, status returns to `open`).
414	
415	Events fired: `drain-paused-quota` is appended to a worker-scoped event
416	log (drain-process record), not to the bead's event stream. A
417	`unclaimed` event is appended to the bead.
418	
419	Claim behavior: the claim on the in-flight bead is released cleanly so
420	another worker (or a later resumed worker) can pick it up.
421	
422	Loop interaction: the worker transitions to worker-state
423	`paused-quota` (section 10). It stops claiming new beads until the
424	configured backoff elapses or an explicit resume signal arrives.
425	
426	### 8.4 RateLimitRetryContract (ddx-c6e3db02)
427	
428	Status transitions used: none. Rate-limit retry is internal to a single
429	attempt; the bead remains `in_progress` throughout.
430	
431	Labels: none.
432	
433	Events fired: `rate-limit-retry` per retry, on the bead's event stream.
434	Each event carries the retry count and the wait duration in the body.
435	
436	Claim behavior: claim is held continuously across retries.
437	
438	Loop interaction: rate-limit handling is bounded by the retry budget; on
439	budget exhaustion the outcome becomes `execution_failed` and the
440	standard mapping in section 5 applies. The worker briefly enters
441	`paused-rate-limit` for the wait window, then returns to `draining`.
442	
443	### 8.5 LockContentionContract (ddx-da11a34a)
444	
445	Status transitions used: none. Lock contention is a store-access
446	concern, not a bead-state concern.
447	
448	Labels: none.
449	
450	Events fired: `lock-contention` (append-only, on the affected bead, only
451	after the contention has been resolved and the mutation succeeded — so a
452	future audit can see that contention was observed). Pure read-side
453	contention does not emit an event.
454	
455	Claim behavior: unaffected. The claim is acquired or not; partial states
456	are not persisted.
457	
458	Loop interaction: the worker retries the locked operation with
459	exponential backoff up to a bounded budget. On budget exhaustion the
460	calling outcome maps to `execution_failed` (section 5). The worker does
461	not enter a dedicated worker-state for lock contention; it remains
462	`draining` and treats the failure as ordinary.
463	
464	## 9. Future-Change Process
465	
466	This is the rule of record for any subsequent change to bead state.
467	
468	> Any bead that introduces or changes a label, an event kind, an outcome →
469	> state mapping, claim handling, or worker-state semantics MUST cite the
470	> TD-031 section that authorizes the change. If no section authorizes it,
471	> TD-031 is amended in the same PR (or a parent bead) before the dependent
472	> work lands.
473	>
474	> Adding a new persisted bead status is **not** authorized by TD-031.
475	> It requires upstream bd/br coordination AND an amendment to ADR-004.
476	
477	In practice:
478	
479	- A new event kind: amend section 4 in the same PR.
480	- A new outcome → state mapping: amend section 5.
481	- A new label: amend section 7.
482	- A new worker state: amend section 10.
483	- A new persisted status: do not start the work; file an ADR-004
484	  amendment first.
485	
486	CI guard: a sibling bead adds a check that any change to
487	`bead-record.schema.json` or to the persisted-status enum touches
488	ADR-004 and TD-031 in the same commit. That guard is out of scope for
489	this bead.
490	
491	## 10. Worker-State Enumeration
492	
493	Worker state is the in-process state of the drain loop. It is **distinct
494	from bead state** and is not persisted in the bead store. It exists only
495	for the worker's lifetime.
496	
497	| Worker state | Meaning | Entry | Exit |
498	|---|---|---|---|
499	| `idle` | Worker is up but not actively claiming. | Worker startup; transient between attempts. | Operator or scheduler moves it to `draining`. |
500	| `draining` | Worker is actively claiming and executing beads. | Default operating state. | Quota or rate-limit pause; explicit stop. |
501	| `paused-quota` | Worker has observed quota exhaustion on its harness. | `drain-paused-quota` event. | `drain-resumed-quota` event after backoff or operator resume. |
502	| `paused-rate-limit` | Worker is sleeping out a rate-limit retry window inside an attempt. | Rate-limit response from harness. | Wait window elapses; worker resumes the same attempt. |
503	| `exiting` | Worker is shutting down cleanly; will not claim more beads. | Operator stop or terminal failure. | Process exit. |
504	
505	Worker state is observable via the worker's stdout/log; it is not stored
506	on any bead. Hygiene beads that affect worker state (QuotaPauseContract,
507	RateLimitRetryContract) MUST NOT add new fields to the bead schema to
508	represent these transient states.
509	
510	## 11. Migration Plan (placeholder)
511	
512	The actual migration survey — which existing beads carry orphaned status
513	values, which code paths persist non-canonical statuses, and what the
514	mechanical rename looks like — runs in the sibling reconciliation bead.
515	This TD does not catalogue current data. The readiness assessment and triage
516	queue-action contract in section 8.2 is already defined here; only
517	compatibility wording such as `MODE: intake` remains legacy.
518	
519	Sibling beads handling the migration:
520

522	  schema; rename code references to non-status names; add CI guards.
523	- Hygiene-bead AC substitution: replace each hygiene bead's
524	  contract section with a reference to TD-031 §8.x.
525	- Migration survey: scan persisted bead stores in known DDx projects for
526	  non-canonical status values and produce a remediation list.
527	
528	When those siblings land, this section is replaced with a concrete
529	migration record citing the sibling bead IDs and the survey result.

292	
293	- **Failure mode clarity.** Oversize inputs produce errors that name the

295	  are classified distinctly from unparseable responses (see §12).
296	
297	- **Backward compatibility.** Bead 0 primitives ship with permissive

306	## User Stories
307	
308	### US-220: Pre-close reviewer succeeds on large changes without silent overflow
309	**As** an operator running `ddx work` against beads with large diffs
310	**I want** the default adversarial pre-close reviewers to produce verdicts on
311	every acceptable diff, and to fail with an actionable overflow error otherwise
312	**So that** primary work is not silently redone after empty reviewer
313	responses
314	
315	**Acceptance Criteria:**
316	- Given a bead whose diff plus governing docs would exceed
317	  `MaxPromptBytes`, when the reviewer runs, then the assembler trims
318	  per §5 and the reviewer sees a bounded prompt
319	- Given trimming cannot reduce the prompt below `MaxPromptBytes`, when
320	  the reviewer runs, then the provider is not called and the bead
321	  receives a `review-error: context_overflow` event with observed and
322	  cap sizes
323	- Given a reviewer invocation emits a recognized verdict, when the
324	  outcome is recorded, then the event body carries `input_bytes`,
325	  `output_bytes`, `elapsed_ms`, `harness`, and `model`
326	
327	### US-221: Operator distinguishes reviewer-failure classes

331	**So that** I know whether to tune prompt caps, retry a transient

337	- Given a reviewer produces text that lacks a verdict line, then the
338	  event summary classifies the outcome as `review-error: unparseable`
339	- Given `ddx agent metrics review-outcomes` is queried, then results
340	  include counts per error class and prompt-size quantiles
341	
342	### US-222: Reviewer failure has a bounded retry ceiling

344	**I want** reviewer failures on the same `result_rev` to stop
345	re-executing primary after a small number of attempts
346	**So that** a single broken bead cannot consume arbitrary tokens
347

353	- Given a `review-manual-required` event exists, when an operator
354	  lists blocked beads, then the bead appears there with the triggering

10	# ADR-024: Power Escalation and Review Routing Policy
11	
12	**Status:** Accepted
13	**Date:** 2026-05-06
14	**Authors:** bead `ddx-5f1eac4f`
15	
16	## Context
17	
18	DDx now has three overlapping concerns in the bead execution path:
19	
20	- retrying a bead attempt when stronger reasoning could plausibly help
21	- running an adversarial pre-close reviewer gate that is independent from the
22	  implementer
23	- enforcing cost visibility and budget stops without taking ownership of
24	  provider/model routing
25	
26	Fizeau owns harness/provider/model routing. DDx owns bead
27	state, worktree isolation, evidence, gates, review verdicts, cooldowns, and
28	the queue-drain loop. The escalation policy therefore has to be expressed in
29	terms of DDx-owned evidence and abstract power bounds, not concrete model names.
30	
31	Earlier planning used "tier" and profile language. That vocabulary is now
32	migration debt when it implies DDx choosing a provider, model, or fallback chain.
33	The stable contract is `MinPower` / `MaxPower` plus opaque passthrough
34	constraints.
35	
36	## Decision
37	
38	DDx power escalation is a DDx retry-policy decision expressed only by changing
39	the next request's `MinPower` and carrying the same request facts forward.
40	DDx does not choose, substitute, downgrade, or fallback concrete harnesses,
41	providers, or models. Fizeau chooses the concrete route.
42	
43	### Primary Attempt Escalation
44	
45	For a primary bead attempt, DDx may schedule a higher-power retry only when all
46	of the following are true:
47	
48	1. a valid attempt started and produced DDx-owned evidence;
49	2. the outcome is classified as plausibly capability-sensitive; and
50	3. no stop condition, hard passthrough constraint, budget cap, cooldown, or
51	   operator-action class has already taken ownership of the result.
52	
53	Eligible capability-sensitive classes include failed post-run checks,
54	implementation-quality failures, review blocks that identify fixable reasoning
55	or design gaps, and no-changes-after-attempt when the model had valid context.
56	
57	Ineligible classes include routing/passthrough conflicts, missing tools,
58	authentication or quota failures, invalid bead metadata, dirty worktree, hard
59	merge/land conflicts that need human judgment, command-not-found setup failures,
60	and any failure where retry would require DDx to inspect or mutate
61	`--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`.
62	
63	When a higher-power retry is allowed, DDx computes the next `MinPower` floor
64	from Fizeau's catalog power numbers. It may skip catalog power floors that have
65	no viable auto-routable model, but it still sends only a new `MinPower` bound
66	to Fizeau. Fizeau chooses the concrete route and reports actual model,
67	provider, and power.
68	
69	### Passthrough Stickiness
70	
71	Operator-supplied passthrough constraints are sticky across every retry.
72	
73	If the operator supplied `--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`, DDx forwards
74	the same values unchanged. If those constraints make a later power request
75	unsatisfiable, DDx records a typed terminal classification such as
76	`blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or `agent_power_unsatisfied` and reports
77	operator action required. DDx must not remove pins, widen pins, substitute a
78	fallback route, call `ResolveRoute` to work around the conflict, or loop over
79	concrete route names.
80

185	and cost summary. No cycle overwrites prior evidence.
186	
187	### Cost Accounting
188	
189	Primary attempts and review attempts both report usage and cost into the same
190	run evidence stream. FEAT-014 defines the normalized cost signal fields.
191	FEAT-010 owns the queue-drain budget stop. Subscription-bundled and local
192	providers may be excluded from billed-cost caps by explicit cost-class metadata;
193	unknown cost class counts by default.
194	
195	Reviewer cost is not special. When a review invocation reports billable cost,
196	that cost contributes to the same drain-level cap as implementation attempts.
197	If the cap is reached, DDx stops claiming more work and records an observable
198	budget stop instead of disguising the stop as model failure.
199	
200	## Consequences
201	
202	- FEAT-006 owns request construction: `MinPower` / `MaxPower`, passthrough
203	  envelope, role/correlation metadata, and actual power recording.
204	- FEAT-010 owns intake, retry scheduling, stop-condition evaluation,
205	  no-progress handling, adversarial review aggregation, review retry scopes, and
206	  budget stops.
207	- FEAT-014 owns normalized usage, cost, cost-class, and freshness semantics.
208	- FEAT-022 and TD-033 own review evidence assembly and no-tool reviewer mode.
209	- DDx routing lint should treat concrete route mutation in `run`, `try`, or
210	  `work` policy as a regression.
211	
212	## Non-Goals
213	
214	- Specifying Fizeau's internal routing, fallback, provider-health, or model
215	  scoring algorithm.
216	- Guaranteeing a different reviewer provider or model. DDx can request stronger
217	  review, dispatch independent reviewer slots, and record degradation, not force
218	  a route.
219	- Retrying by concrete model name or profile mutation.
220	- Adding a fourth run layer or a review-specific execution substrate.
221	
222	## References
223	
224	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md`
225	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-010-task-execution.md`
226	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-014-token-awareness.md`
227	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-022-prompt-evidence-assembly.md`
228	- `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-033-multi-turn-structured-evidence-assembly.md`

11	# Feature: Prompt Evidence Assembly and Size Invariants
12	
13	**ID:** FEAT-022
14	**Status:** Implemented
15	**Priority:** P1
16	**Owner:** DDx Team
17	
18	## Overview
19	
20	DDx constructs prompts for LLM agents from multiple evidence sources: bead
21	metadata, governing documents, git diffs, comparison-arm outputs, user-supplied
22	prompt files, and inline request bodies. Today these assembly paths inline
23	content without size bounds, produce oversize prompts, and in the reviewer's
24	case trigger silent provider-side failures that cascade into redone primary
25	work and wasted tokens.
26	
27	This feature establishes a single repo-wide invariant: **no prompt DDx
28	constructs or accepts may have unbounded size.** It specifies the shared
29	primitives every prompt-building call site must use, the two supported
30	assembly strategies, the failure semantics when inputs exceed caps, and the
31	telemetry needed to observe the system.
32	
33	## Problem Statement
34	
35	**Current situation:**
36	
37	- The post-merge bead reviewer (`BuildReviewPrompt`) inlines full governing
38	  documents and full `git show` diffs without a cap. Large diffs push the
39	  prompt past the reviewer model's context window; some providers return a
40	  hard error, some return zero bytes. DDx surfaces these as `review-error`,
41	  fails to close the bead, and the next worker redoes the primary task —
42	  burning the same large token budget 3–4 times per bead before an operator
43	  intervenes.
44	- The comparison grader (`buildGradingPrompt`) has the same failure shape:
45	  per-arm outputs and diffs are inlined without a cap.
46	- A second review-prompt assembler lives in `cmd/bead_review.go` as
47	  duplicated code, with divergent escape semantics from the agent-side
48	  assembler.
49	- Five call sites (`runner.go`, `compare_adapter.go:181`,
50	  `compare_adapter.go:614`, `service_run.go:83`, `execute_bead.go:1291`)
51	  read `--prompt` files with `os.ReadFile` and no cap. A 100 MB prompt file
52	  reaches the provider unchecked.
53	- Three inline prompt-ingress paths (`/api/agent/run` body `Prompt`,
54	  `DDX_AGENT_PROMPT` env in exec definitions, docgraph prompt metadata)
55	  accept prompt bodies with no cap.
56	- Server text egress (MCP, REST, GraphQL) returns full document, diff,
57	  session, and persona payloads without caps. Any of these responses may
58	  later flow back into a prompt.
59	- No DDx call site has section-level telemetry on prompt assembly. When
60	  reviews fail, diagnosis today requires per-execution bundle archaeology.
61	
62	**Desired outcome:** Every LLM prompt DDx constructs, accepts, or emits
63	through text-returning server endpoints is bounded by explicit caps.
64	Prompt-assembly call sites use shared primitives that enforce caps with
65	clear truncation markers. Oversize inputs fail fast with actionable errors
66	rather than silently corrupting provider calls. Operators see per-section
67	byte accounting on every review and grading attempt.
68	
69	## Requirements
70	
71	### Functional
72	
73	**Shared evidence primitives**
74	
75	1. **Single evidence-assembly package.** DDx provides one package exporting
76	   the primitives every prompt-building call site must use. The package
77	   covers, at minimum, these capabilities:
78	   - Byte-size caps for inlined files, diffs, governing documents, and
79	     total prompts, each with per-harness override resolution.
80	   - Clamped file read (returns content plus amount truncated).
81	   - Clamped text output (bounded length with a canonical truncation
82	     marker).
83	   - Section fitting under a total byte budget with priority-ordered
84	     inclusion. Default behavior is line-based and type-agnostic;
85	     markdown-aware heading extraction is an optional secondary mode.
86	   - Diff clamping and diff decomposition. The decomposer returns file
87	     inventory, per-file stat lines, and hunk headers so large diffs
88	     can degrade to stat + hunk-headers only.
89	   - Two strategy helpers, one for inline-with-cap assembly and one for
90	     ref-only metadata assembly (see §2).
91	   - A canonical truncation-marker string so downstream parsers
92	     recognize truncation uniformly across call sites.
93	
94	   Specific Go identifiers, type shapes, and constant values are defined
95	   in the solution design document for this feature, not in this spec.
96	
97	1a. **Cap defaults and configurability.** Byte-size caps have conservative
98	    defaults shipped in the binary. Project-level overrides are expressed
99	    in `.ddx/config.yaml` under a dedicated evidence-caps section;
100	    per-harness overrides resolve from the same configuration file.

100	    per-harness overrides resolve from the same configuration file.
101	    Defaults and override precedence are defined in the solution design
102	    for this feature. The invariant is that every cap is configurable at
103	    the project level without a rebuild; specific numeric defaults are
104	    not frozen by this spec.
105	
106	2. **Two supported assembly strategies.** Each call site must adopt one:
107	   - **Ref-only metadata** — the primary bead prompt's pattern. Paths and
108	     titles only; agent reads file content via its own tools. Appropriate
109	     when the agent has filesystem access and the prompt does not need to
110	     guarantee the agent saw specific content.
111	   - **Inline-with-cap** — reviewer and grader's pattern. Content is
112	     inlined with bounded size, priority-ordered sections, and truncation
113	     markers. Appropriate when the call must guarantee the agent saw
114	     specific evidence.
115	
116	3. **Repo-wide invariant (no unbounded prompts).** No code path may produce
117	   a value that ends up as an agent-invocation prompt, as a text response
118	   from a server handler that carries library, execution, diff, session,
119	   or persona content, or as an inline prompt body accepted through any
120	   ingress surface, without going through an evidence-package primitive.
121	   Exceptions require an explicit `evidence:allow-unbounded` source
122	   annotation with a justifying comment and are enforced by lint (see
123	   Non-Functional §Lint). Static/literal prompt fragments compiled into
124	   the binary are exempt; the invariant governs dynamic assembly from
125	   runtime inputs, not constant strings.
126	
127	3a. **Static-prompt minimum-prompt rule.** Every static instruction string
128	    DDx embeds in an LLM prompt MUST declare its load-bearing guardrails
129	    inline as a Go comment list above the constant, one bullet per
130	    guardrail with a pointer to the failure mode it prevents. Edits MUST
131	    preserve every listed guardrail. New guardrails MUST be added to the
132	    list. A regression test MUST assert each listed guardrail still
133	    appears in the rendered prompt. This rule applies to STATIC prompt
134	    bodies; dynamic evidence egress is covered by the §5–§7 cap
135	    primitives and is unaffected.
136	
137	**Bounded prompt construction**
138	
139	4. **Single review assembler.** Review prompts are assembled by exactly one
140	   function (`agent.BuildReviewPrompt`). Any duplicate review-prompt
141	   assembler in the CLI command layer is collapsed into this function.
142	   Divergent escape, instruction-template, or section-ordering logic is
143	   reconciled with byte-equivalence tests before the duplicate is removed.
144	
145	5. **Review prompt contents.** The review prompt includes, in priority
146	   order:
147	   1. Bead metadata + acceptance criteria + bead notes (minimum evidence
148	      floor — always present regardless of budget).
149	   2. Changed-file inventory (names + stat lines — minimum evidence floor).
150	   3. Governing document content, bounded per §1. Governing docs that
151	      exceed their cap degrade to heading outline + first N lines.
152	   4. Diff, ranked by relevance (files named in AC text > files matching
153	      governing ref paths > others); per-file cap applies; large files
154	      degrade to stat + hunk headers.
155	
156	6. **Grading prompt contents.** The grading prompt follows the same
157	   bounded-assembly pattern and additionally includes, bounded via
158	   `ClampOutput`, each comparison arm's `PostRunOut`, `PostRunOK`, and
159	   `ToolCalls`. These are first-class evidence fields on the comparison
160	   arm type and must not be silently dropped.
161	
162	7. **Pre-dispatch short-circuit.** When a bounded assembly still exceeds
163	   `MaxPromptBytes` after all trimming, the call site must not dispatch
164	   the provider. Reviewer paths emit a distinct
165	   `review-error: context_overflow` outcome (see §13).
166	
167	**Bounded prompt ingress**
168	
169	8. **Hard-fail on oversize `--prompt` files.** Every call site that reads a
170	   user-supplied prompt file uses `evidence.ReadFileClamped(MaxPromptBytes)`
171	   and hard-fails on oversize with an actionable error message that
172	   includes observed size and cap. Silent truncation is forbidden — a
173	   user-supplied prompt truncated without notice produces incorrect
174	   results the user cannot diagnose.
175	
176	9. **Hard-fail on oversize inline prompt bodies.** The same hard-fail
177	   applies to inline prompt bodies in `/api/agent/run` request bodies,
178	   `DDX_AGENT_PROMPT` environment values, and any other ingress path that
179	   accepts a raw prompt string. Size is checked against `MaxPromptBytes`
180	   before the prompt is handed to the agent.
181	
182	**Bounded server text egress**
183	
184	10. **Universal bounded text responses.** Every server text-returning
185	    handler — MCP tools, REST endpoints, GraphQL resolvers — that emits
186	    library document content, execution bundle content, diff content,
187	    session content, or persona content applies `ClampOutput` and marks
188	    the response as truncated in the response structure when trimming
189	    occurred. The MCP tool response structure, the REST JSON response,
190	    and the GraphQL resolver return type each carry an explicit
191	    `truncated: bool` and `original_bytes` field where applicable.
192	
193	11. **No prompt-adjacent surface is unbounded.** The invariant applies
194	    regardless of whether a specific response is, today, actually consumed
195	    back into a prompt. A bounded-by-default policy prevents new
196	    consumers from reintroducing the overflow class.
197	
198	**Error classes and review policy**
199	
200	12. **Adversarial review evidence contract.** The default close gate assembles
201	    one bounded evidence bundle per `result_rev` and dispatches two no-tool
202	    reviewer invocations. Each reviewer must grade every acceptance criterion
203	    with evidence. An `APPROVE` without per-AC evidence is treated as
204	    `review-error: unparseable`, not as a pass.
205	
206	13. **Review error taxonomy.** The reviewer outcome event distinguishes
207	    four classes:
208	    - `review-error: context_overflow` — bounded assembly exceeded
209	      `MaxPromptBytes` post-trim; provider was not called.
210	    - `review-error: provider_empty` — provider returned zero bytes.
211	    - `review-error: unparseable` — provider returned text but no
212	      recognizable verdict line.
213	    - `review-error: transport` — network or provider error.
214	
215	14. **Reviewer failure preserves the gate.** Reviewer failure must not
216	    close the bead. This invariant (enforced today by
217	    `execute_bead_review_failure_modes_test.go`) is preserved: bounded
218	    prompts change how the reviewer is called, not whether a failed
219	    review can auto-approve.
220	
221	15. **Bounded review retry.** Reviewer invocations are capped at a
222	    configurable N attempts per `result_rev`. On the Nth failure, DDx
223	    emits a terminal `review-manual-required` event, blocks the bead for
224	    operator review, and stops review retries. A new `result_rev` (fresh primary
225	    attempt after manual operator action) resets the counter. Iterations that do not
226	    produce a `result_rev` (e.g. `--no-merge` runs, execution-failed
227	    outcomes) do not consume the review-retry budget; the counter is
228	    scoped strictly to reviewer attempts against a committed result.
229	    This bounds the cost-bleed pattern observed in the filed bug (same
230	    primary task retried 3–4 times) without weakening the gate.
231	
232	**Telemetry and observability**
233	
234	16. **Section-level assembly telemetry.** Review and grading paths
235	    extend the execute-bead attempt bundle (FEAT-014 §19/§20, FEAT-005
236	    §Execute-Bead Evidence Bundle) with an evidence-assembly block
237	    containing:
238	    - per-section bytes (bead / governing / diff / arm-output)
239	    - list of selected vs omitted governing refs
240	    - list of selected vs omitted diff files
241	    - truncation reasons per section
242	    - total `input_bytes` and `output_bytes`
243	    - `elapsed_ms`, `harness`, `model`
244	
245	    Runtime metrics fields already defined by FEAT-014 (`harness`,
246	    `model`, `session_id`, `elapsed_ms`, `input_tokens`,
247	    `output_tokens`, `total_tokens`, `cost_usd`, `base_rev`,
248	    `result_rev`) are not duplicated by FEAT-022; evidence-assembly
249	    telemetry is additive to that record, not a replacement.
250	
251	17. **Compact event summary.** `review`, `review-error`, and
252	    `compare-result` event bodies carry a compact summary
253	    (`input_bytes`, `output_bytes`, `elapsed_ms`, `harness`, `model`).
254	    Full section detail stays in artifacts; event bodies respect the
255	    existing size cap on bead event bodies.
256	
257	17. **Metrics surface.** DDx's existing review-outcomes metrics surface
258	    exposes prompt-size quantiles and the four-class failure breakdown
259	    defined in §12. The specific CLI and API entry points that carry
260	    this data are inherited from FEAT-006 and FEAT-014; FEAT-022 adds
261	    fields, not new commands.
262	
263	18. **Structured assembly topology.** The multi-turn structured-evidence
264	    assembly architecture is specified in TD-033. FEAT-022 owns the
265	    bounded prompt invariants, section ordering, and failure semantics;
266	    TD-033 owns the caller-facing structured evidence envelope and the
267	    no-tool reviewer-mode convention used by FEAT-006 and FEAT-008.
268	
269	### Non-Functional
270	
271	- **Lint (structural sink coverage).** A vet/lint rule, scoped
272	  structurally not by dataflow, enforces the invariant in §3. Rule keys:
273	  - assignment to `RunOptions.Prompt`
274	  - construction of `mcpContent{Type:"text", ...}` literals
275	  - server handlers that write text response bodies from library,
276	    execution, diff, session, or persona data
277	  - readers of `opts.PromptFile` or inline prompt-body request fields
278	  Any flagged path must route through an `internal/evidence` primitive
279	  or carry an explicit `evidence:allow-unbounded` annotation with a
280	  justifying comment.
281	
282	- **Byte-based enforcement and calibration.** All caps are enforced in
283	  bytes. Token-based caps are deferred until DDx has a per-harness
284	  tokenizer binding. Default byte caps are calibrated conservatively
285	  relative to the smallest common-reviewer model's context window using
286	  an assumed worst-case bytes-per-token ratio; the solution design
287	  document carries the concrete calibration table and the per-harness
288	  override mechanism (see §1a). If runtime telemetry (§15) shows that
289	  the default is either rejecting valid prompts or failing to prevent
290	  provider-side overflow, the cap is retuned via configuration, not
291	  via a rebuild.
292	
293	- **Failure mode clarity.** Oversize inputs produce errors that name the
294	  input source, the observed size, and the cap. Empty provider responses
295	  are classified distinctly from unparseable responses (see §12).
296	
297	- **Backward compatibility.** Bead 0 primitives ship with permissive
298	  default caps and cause no behavioral change on land. Tightening caps
299	  and consuming the primitives happens in subsequent beads; each
300	  consumer change is independently reviewable.

302	- **Performance.** Assembly cost per call is bounded by total prompt
303	  size (`O(MaxPromptBytes)`), not by input size on disk. Clamping
304	  occurs at read time; oversize files are not fully loaded into memory.
